traits.description: Lay eggs that can wait out drought, freeze, or famine for years in suspended animation, hatching only when the rains return, the brine-shrimp cysts and resting eggs that let vanished ponds spring back to life.

traits.name: core:complete_metamorphosis
traits.description: Live two utterly different lives: a feeding grub that dissolves itself into soup inside a sealed pupa and reassembles as a winged adult that never eats the same food, the butterfly's total reinvention of the body.

traits.name: core:incomplete_metamorphosis
traits.description: Hatch as a miniature, wingless version of the adult and grow up through a ladder of molts, never pausing to transform, the grasshopper's gradual road from nymph to full-winged imago.

traits.name: core:hypermetamorphosis
traits.description: March through several completely different larval forms in a single childhood, first a nimble hunter that hitches a ride, then a fat sedentary grub, then a resting stage, as blister beetles do to hijack their hosts.

traits.name: core:neoteny
traits.description: Stay a larva forever, breeding while still wearing your juvenile body and feathery gills, never bothering to grow up, the axolotl reproducing in the childhood form its cousins leave behind.

traits.name: core:eusocial_castes
traits.description: Split reproduction from labor: a single queen or a favored few breed while sterile workers and soldiers build, forage, and defend, the whole colony fused into one reproductive body, as in ants, termites, and naked mole-rats.

traits.name: core:horizontal_gene_transfer
traits.description: Acquire genes not from parents but from neighbors and even distant species, swapping loops of DNA like trading cards, the bacterial talent that spreads antibiotic resistance across a hospital overnight.

traits.name: core:transformation_competence
traits.description: Reach out and drink up naked DNA drifting in the environment, stitching a dead cell's genes into your own chromosome, the way some microbes scavenge the genetic remains of their fallen neighbors.

traits.name: core:spore_cloud_panspermia
traits.description: Loft hardened spores by the trillions into the upper atmosphere and beyond, riding dust storms, ejecta, and stellar wind across the void to seed whole new worlds, a lineage that reproduces at the scale of planets.

traits.name: core:genome_fusion_collective
traits.description: When two individuals touch, they melt their genomes into a single chimeric body, then split apart again each richer for the exchange. A species with no fixed self, forever pooling and re-dividing its heredity.

traits.name: core:airborne_gene_dust
traits.description: Shed clouds of genome-rewriting dust that drift on the wind and edit whoever breathes them, so new traits spread sideways through a population like weather, inheritance untethered entirely from birth.

traits.name: core:lunar_mass_spawning
traits.description: Time the entire population's release of gametes to the moon, tide, and temperature, so a whole reef erupts in one snowstorm of eggs on a single night each year, as corals and grunion spawn together beneath the same sky.

traits.name: core:dwarf_parasitic_males
traits.description: Shrink the male to a tiny parasite that bites onto the giant female and fuses with her flesh, dissolving into a permanent sperm organ nourished by her blood, the grotesque devotion of the deep-sea anglerfish.

traits.name: core:sperm_storage
traits.description: Store a single mating's sperm alive for months, years, or a whole lifetime, doling it out to fertilize eggs on your own schedule, the queen bee's decade-long reserve and the patient vault of turtles and bats.

traits.name: core:delayed_hatching_bet_hedging
traits.description: Spread a single clutch's hatching or germination across many seasons, so no single bad year can kill the whole brood, the staggered awakenings of desert seed banks and drought-proof killifish eggs.

traits.name: core:amniotic_egg
traits.description: Seal the embryo inside its own private pond of membranes and a leathery shell, freeing reproduction from open water for the very first time, the reptilian egg that let vertebrates march inland and conquer dry land.

traits.name: core:neuro_diffuse_net
traits.description: A body-spanning mesh of neurons with no head and no boss, as in a sea anemone or comb jelly — every patch of skin senses and answers on its own. Signals ripple outward in slow rings rather than racing to a brain.

traits.name: core:neuro_nerve_ring
traits.description: A loop of nervous tissue circling the body, coordinating radial parts with no center at all — the starfish and jellyfish solution. Decisions emerge from the ring as a whole, so any arm can take the lead.

traits.name: core:neuro_ladder_cord
traits.description: Paired nerve cords running the body's length, rungs of ganglia linking them like a ladder — the segmented plan of earthworms and insects. Each segment can act on reflex while the whole stays in step.

traits.name: core:neuro_giant_axon
traits.description: A single colossal nerve fiber wired for one purpose: instant, whole-body escape, as in a squid's mantle jet. When milliseconds mean life or death, sheer thickness beats finesse.

traits.name: core:neuro_myelination
traits.description: Insulating sheaths wrapped around nerve fibers, letting signals leap down the axon far faster than bare nerves allow — the vertebrate and prawn trick for a big, quick body. Fast wiring is what makes large, snappy animals possible.

traits.name: core:neuro_cephalization
traits.description: Sense organs and nerve clusters crowding toward the leading end, forming a true head — the payoff of moving forward through the world. Where the mouth and eyes go, the brain follows.

traits.name: core:neuro_gap_junction_web
traits.description: Neurons fused by direct electrical bridges, firing in near-instant unison rather than trading slower chemical messengers. It buys blazing speed and tight synchrony at the cost of fine control, as in a crayfish's startle circuit.

traits.name: core:neuro_pattern_generator
traits.description: A self-looping neural circuit that beats out rhythms — walking, breathing, swimming — with no brain needed to conduct. Sever the head and the legs still march, as an insect's do.

traits.name: core:neuro_neurosecretory
traits.description: Nerve cells that double as glands, squirting hormones straight into the blood to steer growth, molting, and mood across days and seasons. The nervous and hormonal systems braided into one, as in an insect's brain-gland axis.

traits.name: core:neuro_encephalization
traits.description: A brain grown lavishly large for the body it steers, gorging on energy in exchange for flexibility — the corvid, primate, and dolphin gamble. Big brains are costly, and only rich, uncertain worlds repay them.

traits.name: core:neuro_cortical_mantle
traits.description: A folded sheet of layered neurons blanketing the brain, packing vast circuitry into a small skull — the mammalian neocortex and its convergent echoes in birds. The more it wrinkles, the more mind it can hold.

traits.name: core:neuro_lateralized_brain
traits.description: Left and right halves specialized for different jobs — one watching for predators, the other minding food — as in fish, birds, and us. Splitting labor between hemispheres lets a creature do two things at once.

traits.name: core:neuro_mushroom_body
traits.description: Dense, cap-shaped learning centers in the insect brain where smells become memories and choices — the fruit fly's and honeybee's seat of learning. Tiny, yet enough to remember a flower or a route.

traits.name: core:neuro_vertical_lobe
traits.description: A cauliflower of neural tissue in the cephalopod brain given wholly to learning and memory, evolved entirely apart from any vertebrate — the octopus's private invention of intelligence. Proof that minds can be built more than one way.

traits.name: core:neuro_arm_minibrains
traits.description: Two-thirds of the neurons pushed out into the arms, each limb tasting, groping, and deciding on its own while the central brain sets only broad intent — the octopus's radically outsourced mind. The body thinks with its hands.

traits.name: core:neuro_reward_modulation
traits.description: Chemical broadcasts of dopamine and its kin that tag some outcomes 'good' and bend behavior toward them — the machinery of wanting, learning, and habit. Every creature that can be trained runs on this.

traits.name: core:neuro_threat_circuit
traits.description: A fast, dedicated alarm system that floods the body with fear and freezes or bolts it before thought catches up — the amygdala's ancient job. Survival often means reacting first and understanding later.

traits.name: core:neuro_nociception
traits.description: Specialized alarm-nerves that register damage as pain, teaching the body to flinch, guard a wound, and shun the fire next time. The unpleasantness is the point — it makes the lesson stick.

traits.name: core:neuro_cognitive_map
traits.description: An inner chart of places and paths, built from special neurons that fire like a living GPS — the hippocampal 'place cells' that let a rat, bee, or bird find its way home. The mind holds a model of the world's shape.

traits.name: core:neuro_split_hemispheres
traits.description: Two brain halves so autonomous they can attend, learn, and even sleep on their own — a bird dozing with one eye and half a brain awake, or the eerie two-minds-in-one-skull of a severed corpus callosum. Sometimes the hemispheres disagree.

traits.name: core:cog_associative_learning
traits.description: Linking one thing to another — a bell to food, a color to poison — so experience reshapes behavior. From sea slugs to mammals, this is the humblest and most universal form of learning.

traits.name: core:cog_circadian_clock
traits.description: An internal oscillator that keeps roughly 24-hour time, priming a creature to wake, hunt, or hide before the sun confirms it. Nearly every nervous system on a spinning world grows one.

traits.name: core:cog_sleep
traits.description: A recurring shutdown when the brain goes quiet to repair, sort, and file the day — a state so vital that even fruit flies and jellyfish do it. No one is quite sure why it is worth the danger of lying senseless.

traits.name: core:cog_dreaming
traits.description: Storms of vivid neural activity in sleep that replay and remix the day into strange inner theater — glimpsed in the twitching paws of dreaming dogs and the sleep-firing of a rat's maze memories. The brain rehearses worlds that aren't there.

traits.name: core:cog_unihemispheric_sleep
traits.description: Sleeping one hemisphere at a time so the other keeps watch and keeps swimming — the dolphin's and frigatebird's trick for resting without ever fully stopping. One eye stays open on the world.

traits.name: core:cog_working_memory
traits.description: A mental scratchpad that holds a few things in mind at once — where the food went, what the rival just did — long enough to act on them. Small in capacity, but the workbench where thinking actually happens.

traits.name: core:cog_memory_consolidation
traits.description: The slow transfer of fresh experience into durable long-term storage, stitched in mostly during sleep. What is learned today becomes who you are tomorrow.

traits.name: core:cog_episodic_memory
traits.description: Recalling specific events as episodes — what happened, where, and when — as a scrub jay remembers exactly which worm it cached and how long ago. Not merely knowing, but re-living.

traits.name: core:cog_prospective_memory
traits.description: Remembering to do something later — to return to a ripening fruit or check a trap at dusk — by projecting the self forward in time. Memory aimed not at the past but at appointments with the future.

traits.name: core:cog_tool_use
traits.description: Turning a found object into an instrument — a rock to crack a nut, a twig to fish for termites, a shell to wear as armor. The world becomes an extension of the body, as for crows, otters, and octopuses.

traits.name: core:cog_tool_making
traits.description: Not merely using objects but shaping them — stripping a twig, knapping a stone, bending a wire into a hook, as New Caledonian crows do. To modify a tool is to hold a plan in mind and impose it on matter.

traits.name: core:cog_planning_foresight
traits.description: Imagining future states and choosing acts to bring them about — caching food for winter, or carrying a tool to where it will be needed. The mind escapes the present tense and rehearses what has not yet happened.

traits.name: core:cog_self_control
traits.description: Overriding the urge for a small reward now to win a larger one later — the marshmallow test that apes, corvids, and cuttlefish can pass. Willpower is a kind of foresight enforced against one's own appetite.

traits.name: core:cog_numerical
traits.description: A sense of quantity — telling more from less, and even keeping a rough tally — that lets a lioness judge a rival pride's roars or a bee count landmarks. The seed of arithmetic, long before any symbol for a number.

traits.name: core:cog_causal_reasoning
traits.description: Grasping that one thing makes another happen — that pulling a string draws in the food, or dropping stones raises the water — rather than merely noticing they go together. Understanding the levers of the world, not just its patterns.

traits.name: core:cog_abstract_reasoning
traits.description: Thinking in categories and relations untethered from any one object — same versus different, bigger than, belongs with. The mind handles ideas as freely as it once handled stones.

traits.name: core:cog_metacognition
traits.description: Knowing what one knows — and sensing the edges of one's own ignorance, so a monkey declines a test it isn't sure of. The mind turns a lamp on itself and reads its own confidence.

traits.name: core:cog_self_awareness
traits.description: Recognizing oneself as a distinct being — the creature that spots a mark on its own body in a mirror and reaches to wipe it away, as great apes, elephants, and magpies do. A mind that knows there is a self to be aware of.

traits.name: core:cog_theory_of_mind
traits.description: Modeling what another creature sees, wants, or believes — and knowing it may differ from your own view, as a raven re-hides a cache from a watcher it thinks saw too much. To predict others, imagine their minds from within.

traits.name: core:cog_deception
traits.description: Deliberately misleading another mind — a false alarm call to steal a meal, a feint, a hidden intention. Once a creature can imagine what others believe, it can also choose to plant beliefs that are false.

traits.name: core:cog_empathy
traits.description: Catching another's feelings and being moved to answer them — consoling a distressed comrade, sharing food with the hungry, as elephants and rats will. The border between self and other softens enough to feel across.

traits.name: core:cog_play
traits.description: Practicing life for its own sake — puppies wrestling, ravens sledding down snowbanks, octopuses tossing a toy in a current. Seemingly useless fun that quietly rehearses the skills of hunting, fighting, and getting along.

traits.name: core:cog_curiosity
traits.description: A hunger to explore and poke at the new for no immediate reward, driving a creature to learn the world before it needs to. The itch that turns a safe animal into an inventive one.

traits.name: core:cog_social_learning
traits.description: Learning by watching others instead of by risky trial and error — where the good foraging is, what is safe to eat. One creature's discovery becomes the whole group's knowledge.

traits.name: core:cog_imitation
traits.description: Copying not just the goal but the very method — the exact motions another used to crack a shell or work a latch. Faithful imitation is the high-fidelity copying that lets skills accumulate rather than fade.

traits.name: core:cog_teaching
traits.description: Actively helping another learn, at a cost to oneself — a meerkat bringing disabled prey to pups and tuning the lesson to their skill. The rare inversion where the knower works so the ignorant may know.

traits.name: core:cog_cultural_transmission
traits.description: Traditions passed down the generations by learning rather than genes — a whale song fashion, a troop's own way of washing food, tool techniques handed from mother to child. Culture lets knowledge outlive the individual and build upon itself.

traits.name: core:cog_vocal_learning
traits.description: Learning to produce new sounds by imitation rather than being born knowing them — the rare gift of songbirds, whales, parrots, and us, and the groundwork of any spoken language. A voice that can be taught can carry culture.

traits.name: core:cog_proto_language
traits.description: A vocabulary of learned signals that stand for things — distinct alarm calls for eagle, snake, and leopard, as vervet monkeys use, or prairie dogs describing an intruder's size and color. Meaning without grammar; words without sentences.

traits.name: core:cog_syntactic_language
traits.description: Combining symbols by rules of grammar into an endless variety of new meanings — the recursive, open-ended language that, on Earth, only one species ever fully crossed into. With syntax, a mind can say things never said before.

traits.name: core:cog_symbolic_thought
traits.description: Letting one thing stand for another — a mark, a gesture, a sound representing an absent object or idea. The mental move that underlies language, counting, art, and every kind of code.

traits.name: core:cog_swarm_decision
traits.description: A colony choosing as one with no leader — scout bees dancing rival nest sites until a quorum tips the whole swarm toward the best. Intelligence that lives in the votes of the many, not the mind of any one.

traits.name: core:cog_hive_mind
traits.description: A colony that computes and remembers as a single distributed mind — an ant nest solving mazes, allocating labor, and waging war though no ant grasps the whole. The superorganism thinks with bodies as a brain thinks with cells.

traits.name: core:xeno_body_wide_cognition
traits.description: Thought with no seat at all — decisions welling up from the whole skin and viscera at once, so the creature has no head to point to and no place a self resides. Cut it in two and each half simply thinks on, a mind smeared evenly through flesh.

traits.name: core:xeno_colonial_superorganism
traits.description: Countless bodies fused into one being that acts with a single will, like a siphonophore whose 'individuals' are merely its organs — a drifting committee that hunts as one. No unit is in charge, yet the whole behaves as though it were.

traits.name: core:xeno_chemical_gradient_compute
traits.description: Solving problems with chemistry instead of neurons — a brainless slime mold that finds the shortest path through a maze and lays out efficient road networks by swelling toward food and shrinking from light. Computation as a slow tide of reacting molecules.

traits.name: core:xeno_bioelectric_field
traits.description: A body-wide electrical hum — standing voltage patterns across the tissues — that stores information and choreographs form and behavior, the way a flatworm's bioelectric map tells its cells where a head should grow. The organism computes in the language of ion gradients, not spikes.

traits.name: core:xeno_mycelial_cognition
traits.description: A sprawling underground web that senses, signals, and reallocates resources across miles of thread — a fungal intelligence with no neurons, routing nutrients and electrical pulses through its living network. It knows its forest the way roots know soil.

traits.name: core:xeno_stigmergic_memory
traits.description: A mind that thinks by writing on the world — laying pheromone trails, scent marks, and built structures that later steer the colony, so memory lives outside any body in the environment itself. The nest remembers what no single member can.

traits.name: core:xeno_regenerative_memory
traits.description: Memory that survives the loss of the brain that formed it — a flatworm trained, beheaded, and regrown, still recalling its lessons in a brand-new head. Knowledge somehow held in the body at large, not the mind alone.

traits.name: core:xeno_quantum_coherent
traits.description: A nervous system that somehow keeps fragile quantum coherence alive in warm tissue, computing across superposed states in ways no classical brain could — a speculative alien mind that would leave physicists arguing for centuries. Thought at the very edge of the possible.

traits.name: core:xeno_crystalline_memory
traits.description: Long-term memories etched as defects in a slowly growing biomineral lattice inside the head, read and rewritten like a living crystal ledger — an alien archive that hardens experience into stone. To forget, it must dissolve part of itself.

traits.name: core:iron_oxidation
traits.description: Prying energy from dissolved iron, oxidizing Fe(II) to rust as Acidithiobacillus and Gallionella do. These microbes laid down the banded iron formations that streak Earth's oldest rocks.

traits.name: core:manganese_oxidation
traits.description: Living off manganese, laying down black oxide crusts, the same chemistry that varnishes desert rocks and lines cave walls. A slow, cryptic way to earn a living from stone.

traits.name: core:sulfur_oxidation
traits.description: Burning hydrogen sulfide and elemental sulfur for energy, storing glittering sulfur granules inside the cell, the trick of Beggiatoa mats and the giant Thiomargarita, a bacterium big enough to see.

traits.name: core:hydrogen_oxidation
traits.description: Breathing hydrogen gas as fuel, the Knallgas reaction, combining H2 with oxygen or other acceptors. A clean, ancient energy source wherever geology leaks hydrogen from the rock.

traits.name: core:ammonia_oxidation
traits.description: The first step of nitrification: oxidizing ammonia to nitrite for energy, as Nitrosomonas does. A cornerstone of the nitrogen cycle that quietly reshapes every soil and sea.

traits.name: core:nitrite_oxidation
traits.description: The second step of nitrification: squeezing energy from nitrite, oxidizing it to nitrate the way Nitrobacter does. The metabolic partner that completes ammonia's journey to nitrate.

traits.name: core:carbon_monoxide_oxidation
traits.description: Making a living from carbon monoxide, a poison to most life. Carboxydotrophs oxidize CO for energy and carbon, thriving near volcanic vents and smoldering ground.

traits.name: core:arsenite_oxidation
traits.description: Turning arsenic into a power source, oxidizing toxic arsenite to arsenate for energy. Life flourishing in the very poison that kills nearly everything else, as in the springs of Mono Lake.

traits.name: core:comammox
traits.description: One organism doing the whole job, ammonia all the way to nitrate in a single cell. The 'comammox' Nitrospira overturned a century of textbook nitrogen cycling.

traits.name: core:methanogenesis
traits.description: Exhaling methane as waste. Archaea fuse hydrogen with carbon dioxide, or split acetate, to make energy and belch out marsh gas. The metabolism that fills wetlands, guts, and deep rock with natural gas.

traits.name: core:acetogenesis
traits.description: Building acetate from thin air. The Wood-Ljungdahl pathway welds two molecules of CO2 together using hydrogen. One of the oldest carbon-fixing tricks known, possibly close to life's very origin.

traits.name: core:reverse_tca_fixation
traits.description: Running the citric acid cycle backwards to build biomass from CO2 instead of burning it, the reductive TCA cycle used by green sulfur bacteria and vent-dwelling Aquificae.

traits.name: core:cam_fixation
traits.description: Breathing in CO2 only at night and locking it away as acid, then photosynthesizing by day with pores sealed shut against the heat. The water-thrifty trick of cacti and pineapples.

traits.name: core:c4_fixation
traits.description: A biochemical supercharger that concentrates CO2 around the enzyme that fixes it, letting grasses like maize and sugarcane photosynthesize furiously in blazing, arid heat.

traits.name: core:photoferrotrophy
traits.description: Photosynthesis powered not by water but by dissolved iron. Ancient purple bacteria used sunlight to strip electrons from Fe(II), rusting the early oceans before oxygen existed.

traits.name: core:rhodopsin_phototrophy
traits.description: A dead-simple solar cell: a single purple pigment protein that pumps ions when struck by light, giving a free energy top-up. Proteorhodopsin colors half the surface ocean's microbes.

traits.name: core:arsenic_photosynthesis
traits.description: Photosynthesis that runs on arsenic instead of water, using sunlight to oxidize arsenite. Discovered in the purple biofilms of arsenic-laced hot springs, life rewriting its chemistry around a poison.

traits.name: core:nitrate_respiration
traits.description: Breathing nitrate when oxygen runs out, stepping it down to nitrogen gas. Denitrification is the anaerobic escape valve that vents fixed nitrogen back to the air.

traits.name: core:sulfate_respiration
traits.description: Breathing sulfate instead of oxygen and exhaling hydrogen sulfide, the rotten-egg reek of Desulfovibrio in black anoxic mud. A dominant way to rot organic matter where oxygen never reaches.

traits.name: core:metal_respiration
traits.description: Breathing solid rock. Geobacter and Shewanella dump their spent electrons onto iron and manganese minerals, respiring metal the way we respire air, sometimes through conductive protein nanowires.

traits.name: core:selenate_respiration
traits.description: Breathing selenium oxides and precipitating tiny red beads of elemental selenium as waste, the rare metabolism that detoxifies and cycles one of the periodic table's stranger elements.

traits.name: core:arsenate_respiration
traits.description: Breathing arsenate, reducing it to even more toxic arsenite for energy, life in the anoxic muds of arsenic-poisoned lakes, turning a deadly element into a respiratory battery.

traits.name: core:sulfur_disproportionation
traits.description: A chemical sleight of hand, splitting a single sulfur compound into both oxidized and reduced products at once and wringing energy from the difference. An inorganic fermentation of the sulfur cycle.

traits.name: core:anammox
traits.description: Anaerobic ammonium oxidation, slamming ammonium and nitrite together to make nitrogen gas, powered by explosive rocket-fuel hydrazine safely caged inside a dedicated organelle. It quietly removes much of the ocean's fixed nitrogen.

traits.name: core:anaerobic_methanotrophy
traits.description: Running methanogenesis in reverse to eat methane without oxygen. Deep-sea archaea team up with sulfate reducers to consume the gas seeping from the seafloor, building towering carbonate chimneys.

traits.name: core:methanotrophy
traits.description: Eating methane with oxygen, the aerobic microbes that intercept marsh gas and seep gas before it reaches the sky. Some build elaborate stacked internal membranes just to hold the enzymes that grab the molecule.

traits.name: core:electrotrophy
traits.description: Eating raw electricity, pulling electrons directly off minerals or a cathode to power the cell with no chemical meal required. Electroautotrophs blur the line between life and battery.

traits.name: core:cable_conduction
traits.description: Centimeter-long living wires. Cable bacteria stack thousands of cells into a filament that carries electrons from sulfide deep in the mud up to oxygen at the surface, splitting one reaction across a vast distance.

traits.name: core:extracellular_respiration
traits.description: Exhaling electrons straight onto the outside world through conductive proteins and nanowires, letting a cell respire surfaces it can't even engulf, the trick behind microbial fuel cells.

traits.name: core:radiotrophy
traits.description: Feeding on radiation itself. Melanin-rich fungi convert gamma rays into metabolic energy, growing toward the shattered reactor core of Chernobyl. Life that treats a lethal dose as a meal.

traits.name: core:radiolytic_feeding
traits.description: An ecosystem with no sun and no surface. Microbes kilometers deep in gold-mine rock live on hydrogen split from water by natural radioactivity, as the lone species Desulforudis audaxviator does.

traits.name: core:thermosynthesis
traits.description: Harvesting energy from temperature swings themselves, a hypothetical primordial metabolism that pumps ions as it cycles between hot and cold, running on the thermal churn of a hydrothermal vent.

traits.name: core:osmotic_gradient_metabolism
traits.description: Living off the boundary where fresh water meets brine, capturing the osmotic pressure released as salt concentrations equalize. A 'blue energy' metabolism at the edge of every salt flat and estuary.

traits.name: core:piezosynthesis
traits.description: An alien metabolism that mines mechanical pressure, coupling the crushing squeeze of the deep to chemical work, thriving where the weight of an ocean or ice shell becomes an energy source.

traits.name: core:serpentinization_feeding
traits.description: Drinking the hydrogen that rock makes when seawater rots olivine, the abiotic fuel welling up at Lost City vents, where life needs no star, only the slow chemistry of cooling mantle.

traits.name: core:nitrogen_fixation
traits.description: Cracking the near-unbreakable triple bond of atmospheric nitrogen into usable ammonia, an ATP-guzzling feat guarded by an enzyme so oxygen-sensitive it must be hidden away. The wellspring of biological nitrogen, from root nodules to cyanobacterial blooms.

traits.name: core:kleptoplasty
traits.description: Stealing the chloroplasts of the algae it eats and keeping them alive under its own skin, running on sunlight for months. The solar-powered sea slug Elysia turns itself into a living leaf.

traits.name: core:endosymbiotic_chemoautotroph
traits.description: Farming chemosynthetic bacteria inside its own tissues and living off their harvest. The giant Riftia tubeworm has no mouth or gut, feeding entirely on sulfur-oxidizing tenants in its blood-red plume.

traits.name: core:hydrogenosome
traits.description: A mitochondrion reinvented for a world without oxygen, an organelle that ferments and puffs out hydrogen instead of breathing air, letting eukaryotes colonize anoxic muds and animal guts.

traits.name: core:syntrophy
traits.description: A metabolism that only works as a partnership, two microbes so tightly coupled that one's waste hydrogen must be whisked away by the other for either to survive. Neither can eat alone.

traits.name: core:photosymbiosis
traits.description: Building a body around a garden of algae. Reef corals, giant clams, and some flatworms house photosynthetic partners in their flesh and split the sugars, a merger that raises whole reefs.

traits.name: core:fermentation
traits.description: Making energy without breathing at all, splitting sugars incompletely and dumping the leftovers as alcohol or acid. The universal fallback metabolism, from yeast in a vat to muscles gasping for air.

traits.name: core:methylotrophy
traits.description: Dining on one-carbon molecules like methanol and methylamine that most life ignores, the scavengers of the simplest organic scraps drifting through soil and sea.

traits.name: core:osmotrophy
traits.description: Eating without a mouth, soaking dissolved organic molecules straight across the cell surface, the diffuse feeding style of fungi, many microbes, and the marine dissolved-carbon economy.

traits.name: core:silicon_biochemistry
traits.description: Life built on silicon in place of carbon, weaving long silicone chains where we use organic ones, a deeply alien chemistry that might arise only in scorching, oxygen-poor worlds where such bonds hold together.

traits.name: core:ammonia_solvent
traits.description: A biochemistry that swaps water for liquid ammonia as the solvent of life, running its reactions dozens of degrees below freezing, the kind of chemistry a frigid ocean world might cradle.

traits.name: core:methane_solvent
traits.description: Life dissolved in liquid methane rather than water, sipping energy from reactions that would freeze solid on Earth, the exotic possibility whispered by the hydrocarbon lakes of Titan.

traits.name: core:sulfuric_acid_solvent
traits.description: A biochemistry armored to live in concentrated sulfuric acid, the searing droplets that make up the clouds of Venus, proposed as a refuge where airborne microbes might drift and metabolize.

traits.name: core:supercritical_co2_solvent
traits.description: An alien metabolism using supercritical carbon dioxide, neither gas nor liquid, as its working fluid, a strange dense medium that could pool in deep high-pressure crust or beneath thick atmospheres.

traits.name: core:selenocysteine_biochemistry
traits.description: Threading selenium into proteins in place of sulfur, building enzymes tuned to seleno-chemistry, an exaggeration of a trick Earth life already uses sparingly in its 21st amino acid.

traits.name: core:arsenic_biochemistry
traits.description: Backbone chemistry that substitutes arsenic for phosphorus in the very scaffolding of life, a possibility once claimed for a Mono Lake microbe. Real or not, it captures how alien a swapped element would be.

traits.name: core:halophily
traits.description: Thriving in brine that would pickle anything else, packing the cell with salts or compatible solutes to survive the Dead Sea and desiccating salterns, where the water itself runs pink with Halobacterium.

traits.name: core:acidophily
traits.description: Flourishing in battery-acid conditions, pumping protons furiously to keep the cell's insides neutral while the world outside runs to pH zero, as in the blood-red acid river of Rio Tinto.

traits.name: core:alkaliphily
traits.description: Living in caustic soda-lake water as alkaline as ammonia cleaner, running a reversed proton economy to cope, the extremophiles of Mono Lake and the East African soda lakes.

traits.name: core:piezophily
traits.description: Built to withstand crushing pressure, with membranes and proteins that only fold correctly under tons of weight, the obligate deep-sea life of the Mariana Trench, which dies if brought up too shallow.

traits.name: core:xerophily
traits.description: Enduring near-total dryness, drifting into suspended animation as its water vanishes and springing back when moisture returns, the anhydrobiosis of desert crusts and the world's driest deserts.

traits.name: core:radioresistance
traits.description: Shrugging off radiation that would shred any other genome, stitching a shattered chromosome back together in hours, the feat of Deinococcus radiodurans, nicknamed 'Conan the Bacterium.'

traits.name: core:endolithic
traits.description: Life inside solid rock, colonizing the pore spaces and cracks microns beneath a stone's surface where a little light or water still reaches, the last refuge in the dry valleys of Antarctica and, perhaps, on Mars.

traits.name: core:metallophily
traits.description: Not merely tolerating heavy metals but soaking them up, hoarding copper, cadmium, or gold to concentrations that would kill anything else, some microbes even precipitating metallic gold grains.

traits.name: core:hyperthermophily
traits.description: Growing hottest where water nearly boils, proteins and membranes engineered to hold together above 100 C, as Pyrolobus and Strain 121 do beside black-smoker vents.

traits.name: core:psychrophily
traits.description: Thriving in the cold that stalls all other life, with antifreeze proteins and supple membranes that keep working below freezing, the microbes of glaciers, brine channels, and the deep sea.

traits.name: core:pentaradial
traits.description: A five-fold body plan radiating around a central axis, like a sea star or sand dollar. Five is a prime that resists splitting evenly, so predators cannot simply halve it — and any arm can lead.

traits.name: core:biradial
traits.description: Two perpendicular mirror planes rather than one, so the body reads the same across two axes but not all — the quiet geometry of a comb jelly gliding on shimmering ciliary rows.

traits.name: core:triradial
traits.description: A three-fold body with no living heir. The Ediacaran trilobozoans spun their organs in threes across the ancient seafloor, a symmetry evolution seems never to have tried again.

traits.name: core:tetraradial
traits.description: Four-fold symmetry, the fourfold canals of a jellyfish's bell visible as a cloverleaf through its glassy dome. Balanced enough to pulse straight through open water.

traits.name: core:hexaradial
traits.description: A six-fold plan, the signature of the stony corals (Hexacorallia) that lay their skeletal septa in sixes. Each polyp a tiny hexagonal cathedral of aragonite.

traits.name: core:octoradial
traits.description: Eight-fold symmetry, the eight feathery tentacles of a soft coral polyp or the eight rays of a sea pen swaying in the current. Octet geometry built for filtering.

traits.name: core:spherical_symmetry
traits.description: Symmetry through a single point, identical in every direction — a radiolarian or a rolling Volvox colony drifting weightless, with no up, down, front, or back.

traits.name: core:helical_body
traits.description: The whole animal is a corkscrew, its axis twisting like a spirochete grown large or an alien that swims by unscrewing itself through water. Handedness is fixed for life.

traits.name: core:directional_asymmetry
traits.description: A bilateral body deliberately lopsided the same way in every individual — a fiddler crab's one giant claw, a flatfish with both eyes migrated to one side, a narwhal's single spiral tusk.

traits.name: core:secondary_bilateral
traits.description: A five-fold body re-imposing a front and a back, as an irregular urchin or a sea cucumber crawls with a chosen head-end — bilaterality reinvented atop radial ancestry.

traits.name: core:toroidal_body
traits.description: An alien donut: the gut loops in a full circle around a central hole with no head and no tail, matter passing through and back into the ring. Rotational symmetry with a void at its heart.

traits.name: core:chiral_handedness
traits.description: Coiling that commits to left (sinistral) or right (dextral). A single gene flips a snail into its mirror image — and mirror snails can barely mate with normal ones, so a flip can split a species.

traits.name: core:metameric_symmetry
traits.description: Translational repetition of near-identical body modules along an axis, like a centipede's endless matched pairs. Copy a segment, then copy it again, and specialize later.

traits.name: core:glide_symmetry
traits.description: Left and right units alternate rather than mirror, offset like footprints or a zipper — the strange quilted body of Ediacaran Dickinsonia, a symmetry with no living descendant.

traits.name: core:torsion_bodyplan
traits.description: The gastropod's contortion: during development the entire visceral mass rotates 180 degrees, twisting the gut into a loop until the anus ends up hanging over the head. Bizarre, and never undone in most snails.

traits.name: core:tensegrity_skeleton
traits.description: Rigid struts float in a continuous web of tension, none of them touching, and the body holds its shape the way a Kenneth Snelson sculpture floats in mid-air. Load spreads everywhere at once.

traits.name: core:silica_spicules
traits.description: Needles of amorphous glass, secreted around protein threads, stiffen the body of a sponge. Each spicule a tiny structural strut of the same silica that makes opal.

traits.name: core:fused_silica_lattice
traits.description: Spicules weld into a single rigid cage of glass, as in the Venus' flower basket — a lattice so precise it channels light like fiber optics and outperforms engineered glass in toughness.

traits.name: core:magnetite_armor
traits.description: The scaly-foot snail plates its foot in iron-sulfide sclerites, and chitons cap their teeth with magnetite, the hardest biomineral known. A body sheathed in ferrous metal, forged from vent chemistry.

traits.name: core:aragonite_skeleton
traits.description: The metastable form of calcium carbonate, favored when the sea's magnesium-to-calcium ratio runs high (an 'aragonite sea'). Corals and most mollusks build with it — denser and glossier, but prone to dissolving back.

traits.name: core:calcite_skeleton
traits.description: The stable carbonate polymorph, favored in low-magnesium 'calcite seas'. Trilobites, brachiopods, and sea-urchin plates all chose calcite — the same mineral ratio in the ocean quietly decides which skeleton a lineage grows.

traits.name: core:celestite_skeleton
traits.description: Acantharians grow star-shaped spines of celestite, strontium sulfate — a mineral so soluble the entire skeleton dissolves the instant the cell dies, leaving no trace in the sediment.

traits.name: core:notochord
traits.description: A turgid dorsal rod that stiffens the body for side-to-side swimming — the founding invention of the chordate line. You carried one yourself, as an embryo, before your spine grew around it.

traits.name: core:vertebral_column
traits.description: The notochord is replaced by a chain of jointed vertebrae, trading a stiff rod for a flexible, protective backbone that shields a nerve cord and anchors the muscles of a powerful swimmer.

traits.name: core:cartilage_skeleton
traits.description: A whole internal skeleton of flexible, collagen-rich cartilage that never ossifies, as in sharks and rays — light, tough, and endlessly flexing, built for a lifetime of swimming.

traits.name: core:bone_skeleton
traits.description: Collagen scaffolds mineralized with hydroxyapatite into a living, remodeling, self-healing internal frame. Bone senses its own load and thickens where it is stressed — a skeleton that redesigns itself.

traits.name: core:pneumatic_bone
traits.description: Bones hollowed out and invaded by air sacs, trussed inside with thin struts like a Warren truss — the weight-saving architecture that helped birds and pterosaurs lift into the sky.

traits.name: core:jointed_appendages
traits.description: Articulated limbs of hollow, hardened tubes worked by muscles slung inside them — the arthropod masterstroke that let animals walk, grasp, dig, and swim on jointed legs long before anything else did.

traits.name: core:molting_exoskeleton
traits.description: A rigid cuticle cannot grow, so the animal periodically splits and sheds it, emerging soft and swelling with air or water before the new shell hardens. The defining, dangerous ritual of the Ecdysozoa.

traits.name: core:chambered_shell
traits.description: A logarithmic spiral divided into sealed, gas-filled chambers threaded by a living tube, the siphuncle, that adjusts their buoyancy — a built-in ballast tank, as in nautilus and the vanished ammonites.

traits.name: core:coiled_univalve
traits.description: A single asymmetric shell wound in a logarithmic helix that the animal carries on its back and retreats into, as snails and limpets do. Grow only at the mouth, and the whole spiral scales with you.

traits.name: core:bivalve_shell
traits.description: Two mirror-image valves joined by an elastic hinge and snapped shut by powerful adductor muscles — clams, mussels, and scallops that can clap their whole skeleton closed in an instant.

traits.name: core:nacre_shell
traits.description: Microscopic aragonite tablets stacked like brick-and-mortar with sheets of protein glue, a laminate so tough that a crack must zig-zag endlessly to spread. It shimmers because those bricks are light-wavelength thin.

traits.name: core:dermal_scutes
traits.description: Bony osteoderms grown directly in the skin, tiling a living animal in armor without a shell — the studded back of a crocodile, the bands of an armadillo, the club at an ankylosaur's tail.

traits.name: core:syncytial_body
traits.description: Thousands of nuclei share one continuous cytoplasm with no walls dividing them — a glass sponge's tissue or a plasmodial slime mold is effectively a single giant cell that can flow, sense, and heal as a whole.

traits.name: core:crystalline_body
traits.description: An alien lineage that grows as an accreting mineral crystal, its metabolism creeping along lattice defects and grain boundaries. It reproduces by cleaving off seed crystals that grow into new individuals.

traits.name: core:fractal_branching
traits.description: A self-similar dendritic form that maximizes surface for the material spent — the repeated forking of a sea fan, a fungal mycelium, or a lung. Zoom in on any twig and the whole plan repeats.

traits.name: core:colonial_zooids
traits.description: A colony of cloned modules that specialize until the whole behaves as one animal — some feed, some sting, some reproduce, some form a float. A Portuguese man o' war is not an individual but a committee of them.

traits.name: core:water_vascular_system
traits.description: A hydraulic network of seawater canals ending in hundreds of muscular tube-feet, each extended by pumping fluid. The echinoderms' unique engine for gripping rock, walking, and slowly prying open a clam.

traits.name: core:stereom_ossicles
traits.description: Each skeletal plate is a single crystal of calcite riddled with a sponge-like mesh of pores, so a sea star's arm is both light and strong and its living tissue threads right through the bone.

traits.name: core:muscular_hydrostat
traits.description: A boneless, fluid-free organ of interwoven muscle that keeps constant volume, so shortening it one way makes it bulge another. An octopus arm, an elephant's trunk, and your own tongue all move this way.

traits.name: core:agglutinated_test
traits.description: Rather than secreting mineral, the cell cements a shell from scavenged sand grains and sponge spicules, sorting and gluing its building blocks one at a time. Some foraminifera are pickier about their grit than a mason.

traits.name: core:coccolith_armor
traits.description: A single-celled alga sheathes itself in interlocking discs of calcite it manufactures inside its own body and pushes to the surface, tile by tile. Blooms of these built the White Cliffs of Dover.

traits.name: core:radiolarian_skeleton
traits.description: A single cell spins a geodesic sphere of opaline silica, nested lattices within lattices bristling with radiating spines — a microscopic cathedral of glass built by a blob of cytoplasm.

traits.name: core:bouligand_armor
traits.description: Fiber layers each rotated a few degrees from the one below form a molecular plywood that blunts cracks by forcing them to corkscrew. A mantis shrimp's dactyl club survives thousands of hammer-blows because of it.

traits.name: core:sclerites
traits.description: Discrete hard elements — spines, scales, ossicles, hooks — studded through otherwise soft tissue, like the hidden grit inside a sea cucumber or the chain-mail of Cambrian Wiwaxia. Armor without committing to a full shell.

traits.name: core:fused_carapace
traits.description: Many separate plates knit into a single rigid shield over the body — a turtle's shell fused to its own ribs, a crab's carapace, a trilobite's headshield. One-piece armor at the cost of flexibility.

traits.name: core:fused_test
traits.description: A sea urchin's ossicles interlock into a single rigid globe studded with ball-and-socket joints for its spines — a geodesic dome of living calcite that grows without ever unsealing.

traits.name: core:gas_float_skeleton
traits.description: A chitin-walled gas bladder becomes the load-bearing element, a float from which the whole colony hangs and drifts — the Portuguese man o' war's sail, Velella's little raft riding the wind across the sea.

traits.name: core:cellulose_tunic
traits.description: A sea squirt wraps itself in tunicin, a leathery cellulose coat — making tunicates the only animals that build their bodies from the very polymer plants use for wood.

traits.name: core:quasicrystal_skeleton
traits.description: An alien skeleton grown as an aperiodic quasicrystal — five-fold order that fills space without ever repeating, a structure Earth chemistry can barely coax into a lab. Here it is grown, patiently, as living scaffold.

traits.name: core:ferrofluid_endoskeleton
traits.description: An alien body stiffened by a suspension of magnetic particles that snaps rigid or flows limp on command, as internal field-generating organs pulse. A skeleton with no fixed shape, hardening only where and when it is needed.

traits.name: core:collagen_matrix
traits.description: Triple-helix protein rope woven into a tough yet flexible composite — the near-universal animal scaffold that holds tissues together and gives every later biomineral something to grow upon.

traits.name: core:catch_connective_tissue
traits.description: Echinoderm connective tissue whose stiffness the nervous system dials in seconds, locking a sea cucumber rigid as a rock or letting it pour through a crack like liquid — all at almost no muscular energy cost.

traits.name: core:byssal_anchor
traits.description: A mussel spins tough protein cables from a gland in its foot and cements them to bare rock with an underwater adhesive that shrugs off pounding surf — a living anchor line no glue engineer has yet matched.

traits.name: core:operculum
traits.description: A hardened disc carried on the foot that seals the shell mouth like a trapdoor the moment the animal withdraws, locking out predators, drought, and cold behind a perfect stopper.

traits.name: core:spiral_phyllotaxis
traits.description: Modules placed one golden angle apart spiral into interlocking Fibonacci curves, packing seeds or scales with no gaps and no crowding — the mesmerizing double spiral of a sunflower head, a pinecone, a cactus.

traits.name: core:antler_horn
traits.description: Bony antlers grown and cast off every year, or permanent keratin-sheathed horns — headgear for combat and courtship that can outweigh the rest of the skeleton and rewrite a whole animal's posture.

traits.name: core:hydroskeleton_septa
traits.description: A fluid skeleton walled into compartments so muscles can pressurize one segment without the whole body going soft — how an earthworm marches its bristles forward wave by wave, and an anemone reshapes at will.

traits.name: core:crossed_lamellar_shell
traits.description: Aragonite laid down in herringbone plies angled against one another, the toughest known mollusk-shell microarchitecture. A conch shell shrugs off a hammer-blow by splitting the crack across a thousand tiny grains.

traits.name: core:prismatic_shell_layer
traits.description: A palisade of calcite columns forms a hard, chalky outer shell layer, usually built over a nacre lining — the two-tone sandwich of a pearl oyster's valve, tough outside and iridescent within.

traits.name: core:living_lattice
traits.description: An alien organism built of identical structural cells that latch and release on cue, so the whole body can rebuild its truss, sprout a limb where it needs one, or dissolve into a crawling swarm and reassemble elsewhere.

traits.name: core:opal_lens_skeleton
traits.description: A brittlestar's arm plates bristle with thousands of flawless calcite microlenses that focus light onto nerves beneath — a skeleton that is also a compound eye, letting a limb see without a head.

traits.name: core:endosymbiont_capture
traits.description: The fateful engulfing of one microbe by another that ends not in digestion but in partnership, the captive kept alive as a live-in tenant. Like the ancient merger that founded every complex cell, it is evolution's boldest gamble: swallow a stranger and make it family.

traits.name: core:mitochondrial_symbiosis
traits.description: An engulfed aerobic bacterium tamed into a private powerhouse, burning oxygen to flood its host with energy. Like the mitochondrion at the root of all animals, fungi, and plants, this one captured guest paid for the whole extravagant future of large, active life.

traits.name: core:plastid_symbiosis
traits.description: A captured cyanobacterium kept alive inside the cell as a sun-harvesting organelle, turning a consumer into a producer in a single swallow. This is the chloroplast's origin, the merger that gave rise to algae and every green plant that followed.

traits.name: core:secondary_endosymbiosis
traits.description: Swallowing a creature that had already swallowed a photosynthetic partner, so plastids end up nested three or four membranes deep, a partnership folded inside a partnership. This Russian-doll capture built the cryptophyte and dinoflagellate algae.

traits.name: core:nitrogen_fixer_symbiosis
traits.description: Housing nitrogen-fixing bacteria in root nodules or dedicated cells, buying scarce fertilizer with a steady tithe of sugar. Like legumes and their Rhizobium, this pact lets its keepers green barren, starved ground where nothing else can gain a roothold.

traits.name: core:photosymbiosis_zooxanthellae
traits.description: A stationary animal farming photosynthetic algae inside its own flesh, trading shelter and carbon dioxide for a constant drip of sugar. This is the engine of coral reefs, an animal that eats sunlight secondhand through the guests glowing in its tissues.

traits.name: core:reef_framework_builder
traits.description: Colonies of algae-farming animals laying down mineral skeletons layer on layer, cementing over ages into vast living reefs. Architecture built by symbiosis, these structures house a third of all ocean life in the sunlit shallows they raise from the seabed.

traits.name: core:symbiont_bleaching_tolerance
traits.description: The desperate reset switch of a photosymbiotic host: expel your algal tenants under heat or stress and survive pale and half-starved until new ones can be recruited. Corals do this when the water turns hostile, gambling a bleached famine against certain death.

traits.name: core:giant_clam_symbiosis
traits.description: A huge sessile filter-host splaying iridescent, lens-studded mantle flesh to the light, gardening algae in its exposed tissue. Like Tridacna clams that swell to a meter across fed largely by their farmed guests, it grows its shell around a captive sun-garden.

traits.name: core:chemosymbiosis_trophosome
traits.description: Housing sulfur- or methane-oxidizing bacteria in a dedicated organ and dissolving the gut entirely, letting chemical-eating tenants do the feeding. Like the mouthless giant tubeworm Riftia at deep-sea vents, its whole body is a garden for microbes that eat poison.

traits.name: core:vent_symbiont_transmission
traits.description: Handing the essential chemosynthetic partners to the next generation, seeded into eggs or recaptured fresh from the water, so each larva can colonize a new vent and rebuild the alliance from nothing. Without this handoff the whole mouthless lineage would end in one dark generation.

traits.name: core:methane_seep_symbiosis
traits.description: Gills packed with methane-oxidizing bacteria let mussels and clams graze cold seeps where methane bubbles up through the seafloor mud, breathing food out of what should be a lethal gas. Whole communities cluster where the planet exhales.

traits.name: core:cleaning_mutualism
traits.description: One species grooming parasites, dead skin, and food scraps off another, and both walking away better for it. Like cleaner wrasses working a client fish's open mouth or oxpeckers riding a rhino's back, it is trust rendered as a service exchanged between strangers.

traits.name: core:cleaning_station_fidelity
traits.description: Cleaners holding a fixed post that clients return to again and again, honesty enforced by reputation. Dance displays and gentle reassuring touches advertise safe service, and a cleaner that cheats and bites finds its custom drying up.

traits.name: core:nectar_reward_syndrome
traits.description: The plant's half of the pollination bargain: nectar spurs, perfume, bright landing-pad petals, and pollen packaged to hitch a ride. Every color, scent, and shape evolved to bribe a courier and steer it toward the next flower of the same kind.

traits.name: core:obligate_pollination_pact
traits.description: A plant and a single pollinator locked so tightly that neither can reproduce without the other, a coevolved marriage no third party may enter. Like figs and their fig wasps, each partner has thrown away every other option and bet its entire lineage on the other.

traits.name: core:brood_site_pollination
traits.description: The pollinator laying its eggs inside the very flower it fertilizes, its larvae eating a measured share of the seeds as payment. The yucca moth and fig wasp walk this razor's edge, where taking too many seeds would starve the partnership that feeds them.

traits.name: core:mycorrhizal_network
traits.description: Fungal threads sheathing plant roots and trading scavenged minerals and water for photosynthetic sugar, wiring whole forests into a shared underground market. This is the wood wide web, where a dying tree can pump its last carbon to seedlings through the fungal grid.

traits.name: core:root_graft_network
traits.description: Neighboring plants fusing roots into one common circulatory grid, pooling water and sugar so a whole stand lives and dies as a single organism. Quaking aspen clones do this across acres, a forest that is really one enormous connected individual.

traits.name: core:mycoheterotrophy
traits.description: A plant that quit photosynthesis and turned cheat, siphoning sugar out of the fungal network it was once meant to feed. Ghostly, leafless, waxen things like the Indian pipe rise from the forest floor to steal from the shared bank, giving nothing back.

traits.name: core:lichen_partnership
traits.description: A fungus and an alga or cyanobacterium fusing so completely they become a single tough new organism with its own shape and name. These pioneers colonize bare rock and endure searing sun and hard frost, two lives welded into one that neither could sustain alone.

traits.name: core:poikilohydric_dormancy
traits.description: Drying to a brittle, metabolically dead crisp in drought and springing back to life within minutes of rain. This lichen-and-moss trick laughs at deserts, bare stone, and even the vacuum of space, riding out the impossible by simply switching off until conditions relent.

traits.name: core:gut_microbiome_farming
traits.description: Cultivating a private ecosystem of gut microbes that ferment tough plant fiber into digestible fuel. These hidden partners are what let termites eat dead wood and cattle live on grass, unlocking foods the host's own enzymes could never touch.

traits.name: core:foregut_fermentation
traits.description: A multi-chambered fermenting vat up front where microbes break down fiber before true digestion begins. The ruminant's four-stomach solution regurgitates the half-brewed mash as cud to chew again, wringing the last calorie out of the toughest forage.

traits.name: core:coprophagy_reinoculation
traits.description: Eating one's own droppings to reseed the gut fauna and squeeze a second pass of nutrition from already-processed food. Rabbits and many insects farm their microbes twice this way, running each meal through the fermenter loop until nothing usable remains.

traits.name: core:fungus_farming
traits.description: A society that gathers substrate, plants fungal spores on it, weeds and fertilizes the crop, and eats the harvest. Leafcutter ants and termites invented agriculture tens of millions of years before humans, tending sterile monoculture gardens deep in their nests.

traits.name: core:aphid_ranching
traits.description: Herding another species like livestock, stroking aphids to milk their sugary honeydew, guarding them from predators, and carrying them to fresh pasture. Some ants even overwinter aphid eggs deep in the nest, tending a domesticated herd across the generations.

traits.name: core:livestock_domestication
traits.description: An intelligent species that captures, breeds, and manages whole populations of a mobile prey animal as a controlled food stock, a rare leap from hunting to husbandry. It reshapes another creature's very body over generations to serve as walking, self-replenishing meat.

traits.name: core:social_parasitism_brood
traits.description: Sneaking your young into another society to be raised at its expense. Like the cuckoo that dupes a smaller bird or the bee that lays in a stranger's nest, the impostor lets its unwitting host pour care into a chick or grub that is not its own.

traits.name: core:dulosis_slavemaking
traits.description: Raiding rival colonies to carry off their brood, which hatch into a captive workforce toiling for their captors. The amazon ants are so committed to this piracy they can no longer feed themselves, and starve amid plenty without their kidnapped slaves.

traits.name: core:queen_usurpation
traits.description: An invading queen slipping into a foreign colony, assassinating the resident monarch, and duping the orphaned workers into raising her own brood atop the corpse of their line. The colony toils on, never sensing its whole future has been quietly hijacked.

traits.name: core:phoresy_hitchhiking
traits.description: Clamping onto a larger, faster species purely to travel, freeloading on someone else's mobility. Mites grip a beetle's leg, a remora rides a shark's flank, and pseudoscorpions catch a ride under a fly's wing, all covering distance they could never cross alone.

traits.name: core:commensal_shelter
traits.description: Lodging in or on another's home or body for protection without paying rent or doing harm. Barnacles cement to a whale's skin, and a small fish shelters unbothered among an anemone's stinging tentacles, safe in a fortress it did not build.

traits.name: core:inquilinism
traits.description: Taking up permanent residence inside another living creature's body cavity as a harmless lodger. The pearlfish shelters within a sea cucumber's gut, slipping in and out through its host's back door, a tenant in a home that breathes and moves.

traits.name: core:cooperative_pack_hunting
traits.description: Hunting as a coordinated team with assigned roles, drivers and blockers and ambushers, to bring down prey no lone hunter could. Wolves, lions, and orcas think together to kill, sharing the labor of the chase and, usually, the meat at its end.

traits.name: core:interspecific_hunting_alliance
traits.description: Two entirely different species teaming up to hunt and split the spoils. A grouper and a moray flush prey from opposite sides of a reef; a honeyguide leads a badger or a human to a bee nest and takes its cut of the wax, cooperation reaching across the tree of life.

traits.name: core:cooperative_web_colony
traits.description: Hundreds of individuals spinning and sharing one enormous communal snare, then swarming together onto prey far too large for any single hunter. The rare social spiders pool their silk and their venom, a colony that hunts as one vast many-legged trap.

traits.name: core:superorganism_colony
traits.description: A colony so integrated it behaves as one body, its members unable to live or breed alone. The hive or nest becomes the true organism and each ant or bee is merely one of its cells, a single mind and a single life spread across thousands of bodies.

traits.name: core:physically_integrated_colony
traits.description: Budded individuals that never separate, fused into one body sharing tissue and food while specializing into swimmers, feeders, and stingers. The siphonophore, that drifting Portuguese man o' war, is not one animal but a colony welded into the shape of one.

traits.name: core:nest_homeostasis
traits.description: The colony collectively holding its nest at a steady temperature, humidity, and airflow. Termite mounds vent through chimney towers, and bees fan and shiver in coordinated shifts to air-condition the hive, thousands of bodies acting as one climate-control organ.

traits.name: core:trophallaxis_sharing
traits.description: Mouth-to-mouth passing of food and chemical messages, circulating a shared social stomach and a common scent through the whole colony. This constant liquid exchange binds thousands of separate bodies into one metabolic and informational whole.

traits.name: core:caste_polymorphism
traits.description: Siblings from the same eggs developing into radically different bodies, tiny nurses, huge-jawed soldiers, winged royals, each shaped for a single job. The division of labor is written into flesh itself, a society whose roles are baked in at birth.

traits.name: core:soldier_caste
traits.description: A dedicated warrior form with weaponized anatomy: massive shearing jaws, a plugged armored head that corks the nest door, or a turret that sprays glue and poison. Bred solely to defend, these soldiers often cannot even feed themselves, living only to fight.

traits.name: core:reproductive_division
traits.description: A sterile working majority surrendering breeding entirely to a single royal, raising siblings instead of offspring. It is the ultimate altruism, where most of the colony never passes on its own genes and instead spends its whole life serving the one that does.

traits.name: core:replete_caste
traits.description: Living pantries: workers gorged with food until their abdomens swell to translucent golden globes, hanging motionless from the nest ceiling to be milked by their sisters in lean months. The honeypot ants keep such storage-bodies, larders made of their own kin.

traits.name: core:swarming
traits.description: Massing into dense, synchronized clouds or columns that move and turn as one. Locust plagues darken the sky, starling murmurations fold and pour like smoke, and the sheer coordinated number becomes both a shield against predators and a terror to everything below.

traits.name: core:density_phase_shift
traits.description: Crowding flips a hidden switch, transforming shy, drab, solitary individuals into bold, brightly marked, gregarious ones within a single generation. The Jekyll-and-Hyde change that turns quiet grasshoppers into marching locust armies is triggered simply by the touch of too many neighbors.

traits.name: core:quorum_sensing_bloom
traits.description: Individuals broadcasting a chemical tally of their own numbers and acting only once the crowd grows large enough. The bacterial vote suddenly lights a whole colony, triggers an algal bloom, or launches a mass attack in perfect unison, a decision made by concentration alone.

traits.name: core:seasonal_migration
traits.description: Whole populations journeying vast distances on a seasonal clock to chase food, water, or breeding grounds. Wildebeest rivers thunder across the plains and skies fill with birds tracking the turning year, an entire species moving as one great tide.

traits.name: core:multigenerational_migration
traits.description: A migratory circuit so long that no single individual completes it; the route is run in relay across three or four generations, each one hatching, flying its leg, and dying, the inherited map somehow passed on. Monarch butterflies loop the continent this way, guided by ancestors they never met.

traits.name: core:natal_homing
traits.description: Navigating back across oceans or continents to the exact place of one's birth to breed. Salmon climb the very stream they hatched in and sea turtles crawl ashore on their natal beach decades later, carrying a map of home written before they could see.

traits.name: core:tissue_fusion_collective
traits.description: An alien sociality where separate individuals routinely graft their bodies together into one shared-flesh collective, pooling organs and circulation, then peel apart at will. The self becomes fluid, sometimes one great creature, sometimes a scattering of many, boundaries dissolving and reforming by the hour.

traits.name: core:chemical_consensus_mind
traits.description: A brainless society that thinks by chemistry, each member releasing scent-votes that diffuse and tally until a threshold tips the whole group into a single choice. It is a diffuse democracy with no ruler and no mind at its center, decisions condensing out of the air like weather.

traits.name: core:obligate_endodweller
traits.description: A creature that can live nowhere but inside another's living body, born, fed, and mated within a host's tissues without ever killing it. Its entire universe is the warm dark interior of a partner it never leaves, a life lived wholly inside someone else.

traits.name: core:host_body_architecture
traits.description: The host evolving purpose-built interior real estate for its guests: dedicated crypts, ducts, feeding chambers, even light-piping windows to feed captive glowing bacteria. It remodels its own anatomy into a habitat, a body redesigned from within to house the species it keeps.

traits.name: core:bioluminescent_symbiosis
traits.description: Housing glowing bacteria in a special organ and spending their cold light to signal, lure prey, or erase your own shadow against the moonlit surface above. The bobtail squid hides in borrowed glow and the anglerfish dangles a living lantern, fire farmed from tenants.

traits.name: core:mind_merge_plexus
traits.description: Alien minds that physically splice their nervous systems together to pool cognition into a temporary group super-brain, solving as one and remembering as one, then unplug and scatter. Each departs carrying only a fragment of what the whole once knew, thoughts that existed only while joined.

traits.name: core:chimeric_holobiont
traits.description: An organism that is really a permanent federation of dozens of unrelated species woven into one working body, with no single self but a walking, breathing ecosystem. It moves, feeds, and reproduces as though it were one creature, though it is a whole community wearing the mask of an individual.

traits.name: core:altruistic_alarm
traits.description: Risking your own life to warn your kin, a shrieked alarm call that draws the hawk's eye or an alarm pheromone that scatters the herd while the caller stands exposed. The sacrifice is paid in the hidden currency of shared genes, a life spent to save many like it.

traits.name: core:allogrooming_bonding
traits.description: Grooming one another far beyond mere hygiene, trading long sessions of cleaning to build alliances, soothe tensions, and cement rank. This patient touch is the social glue of primate troops and many a tight-knit band, friendship rendered as fingers combing fur.

traits.name: core:cooperative_brood_care
traits.description: Non-parents pitching in to feed, guard, and teach another's young. The helpers-at-the-nest of meerkats and scrub jays give up breeding of their own to babysit siblings, and an extra pair of watchful eyes can matter more to the family than another clutch of eggs.

traits.name: core:eusocial_vertebrate_analog
traits.description: True insect-style eusociality erupting in a warm-blooded, backboned lineage: sterile workers, a single breeding queen, and lifelong underground castes. The naked mole-rat walks this improbable path, a colony of mammals living like ants beneath the desert.

traits.name: core:keratin_integument
traits.description: A tough, water-resistant skin woven from cross-linked keratin proteins, the same material as your fingernails and a rhino's horn. It seals in moisture and shrugs off abrasion, forming the foundation from which scales, claws, and quills are later built.

traits.name: core:mucus_slime_coat
traits.description: A slick blanket of glycoprotein slime coating the entire body, as on a hagfish or a garden slug. It resists infection, lets the animal slither from a predator's grip, and can be thickened into choking gel in an instant.

traits.name: core:defensive_exocrine_gland
traits.description: Specialized skin glands that brew and store noxious cocktails, discharging them on demand. This single innovation opens the door to venoms, irritant sprays, sticky glues, and stinking repellents across the tree of life.

traits.name: core:waxy_epicuticle
traits.description: A microscopically thin varnish of waxes and lipids over the cuticle, the trick that let insects and arachnids conquer dry land. It waterproofs the body so completely that a beetle can cross a desert without drying to a husk.

traits.name: core:neurotoxin_venom
traits.description: A venom that jams the nervous system, blocking ion channels so nerves misfire and muscles seize, as in a cone snail or a black mamba. A minute dose can paralyze prey or drop an attacker before it lands a blow.

traits.name: core:hemotoxin_venom
traits.description: A venom that assaults blood and tissue, wrecking cells and preventing clotting so the wound bleeds and rots, like a rattlesnake or a recluse spider. Slower than neurotoxin but devastating, it liquefies prey from the inside out.

traits.name: core:cytotoxin_venom
traits.description: A cell-dissolving venom that kills whatever tissue it touches, spreading necrosis outward from the wound, as spitting cobras and many spiders wield. Cruelly effective at deterring anything that tries to eat or seize its owner.

traits.name: core:envenoming_fangs
traits.description: Hollow or grooved fangs plumbed to venom glands, a hypodermic syringe grown from teeth. Snakes and spiders drive them home to inject their brew deep past skin and scale.

traits.name: core:barbed_stinger
traits.description: A sharp, often barbed spine at the body's rear fed by a venom sac, wielded by scorpions, bees, and stingrays. Some tear free and keep pumping venom even after their owner has fled.

traits.name: core:bombardier_spray
traits.description: Twin chambers slam reactant chemicals together in an explosive reaction, firing a boiling, caustic jet with an audible pop, exactly as a bombardier beetle does. The scalding spray can be aimed with startling precision at a probing snout.

traits.name: core:formic_acid_spray
traits.description: Glands that spray or weep concentrated formic acid to burn and repel attackers, as ants and some caterpillars do. A fine mist can drive off a bird or corrode the joints of a rival insect.

traits.name: core:caustic_alkaloid_slime
traits.description: Body slime laced with burning alkaloids and saponins that blister a predator's mouth, the strategy of many toxic newts and sea slugs. A single taste teaches a lifelong lesson.

traits.name: core:sticky_entangling_secretion
traits.description: A gland that ejects threads or globs of instantly tacky glue to snare an attacker's limbs and gum up its jaws, like a velvet worm's slime cannon. The victim struggles uselessly while the sprayer strolls away.

traits.name: core:sulfurous_stink_gland
traits.description: Glands that unleash an overwhelming sulfurous or musky stench to foul the air and send predators reeling, as skunks, stink bugs, and stinkpot turtles do. The reek can cling to an attacker for days as a walking warning.

traits.name: core:regurgitant_deterrent
traits.description: The animal vomits a foul slurry of digestive juices and stored toxins onto itself or an aggressor, as fulmar chicks and many caterpillars do. Repulsive, sticky, and often poisonous, it ruins a predator's appetite for good.

traits.name: core:dietary_toxin_sequestration
traits.description: Rather than making its own poisons, the animal harvests them from toxic food and stockpiles them in its own tissues, as monarch butterflies hoard milkweed cardenolides. A cheap way to become inedible simply by eating danger.

traits.name: core:poison_dermal_alkaloids
traits.description: The skin itself is saturated with potent alkaloids, so a single lick or bite delivers a dose of poison, as in a golden poison frog whose skin holds enough to kill ten people. Utterly harmless until touched, then lethal.

traits.name: core:symbiont_toxin_farming
traits.description: The creature cultivates colonies of poison-making microbes in specialized skin crypts, outsourcing its chemical arsenal to tenant bacteria, echoing how some newts and octopuses borrow tetrodotoxin from microbial partners. On alien worlds whole guilds of bespoke toxin-brewers may be farmed side by side.

traits.name: core:dermal_ossicles
traits.description: Bony plates and nodules grown within the skin itself, forming a flexible armor over the body as in crocodiles, armadillos, and sea cucumbers. Tough as tile yet jointed enough to let the animal keep moving.

traits.name: core:silica_spicule_armor
traits.description: A dense lattice of microscopic glass needles grown from dissolved silica, stiffening the body and lacerating anything that bites down, as in glass sponges and some sea stars. The spicules can knit into structures stronger than engineered glass.

traits.name: core:keratin_quills
traits.description: Hairs or scales thickened into sharp, barbed quills that drive into an attacker and detach, as a porcupine's do. A mouthful of them can cripple or kill a careless predator.

traits.name: core:urticating_bristles
traits.description: Fine, brittle hairs tipped or filled with irritants that lodge in skin and airways, flicked into the air by tarantulas or shed by caterpillars. They itch, burn, and inflame for days, an invisible cloud of misery.

traits.name: core:defensive_spines
traits.description: Rigid spines and thorns projecting from the body surface, from a sea urchin's test to a horned lizard's crown, turning the animal into an unswallowable mouthful of points. Some lock erect the instant danger nears.

traits.name: core:interlocking_scute_armor
traits.description: Overlapping horny plates that tile the body like shingled mail, as on a pangolin or a tortoise's shell, deflecting teeth and claws while still bending at the seams. A predator's jaws slide off with nothing to grip.

traits.name: core:calcified_body_plates
traits.description: Heavy plates of calcium mineral fused into a rigid box around the body, the strategy of boxfish, glyptodonts, and armored catfish. Nearly impregnable, at the cost of speed and grace.

traits.name: core:living_armor_symbiont
traits.description: The body's surface hosts a crust of living symbiotic organisms, encrusting shells, stinging polyps, or plated microbes, that serve as regenerating, self-repairing armor. Where a decorator crab merely glues on borrowed camouflage, these partners are grown, fed, and healed as part of the body.

traits.name: core:dermal_denticles
traits.description: Tooth-like scales of enamel and dentine studding the skin, giving a shark its sandpaper hide and its silent, drag-cutting glide. They armor the body and shred anything that rubs against the grain.

traits.name: core:iron_sulfide_scale_armor
traits.description: Scales sheathed in iron minerals scavenged from the water, the astonishing trick of the scaly-foot snail of the deep-sea vents, whose foot is clad in plates of iron sulfide. It is the only animal known to armor itself in metal.

traits.name: core:regenerating_shell_mosaic
traits.description: Armor built from thousands of small mineralized tiles that continuously regrow and reseat as they crack, so damage patches itself within hours. No Earth animal repairs armor this fast; the surface shimmers as tiles shuffle to cover a fresh wound.

traits.name: core:ink_cloud_screen
