traits.description: A sac of dark pigment ejected as a billowing cloud that blinds and confuses a pursuer, the classic escape of squid and octopus. Some blast a mucus-bound blob shaped like their own body, leaving a decoy hanging in the water.

traits.name: core:noxious_gas_discharge
traits.description: A vent that releases a choking cloud of volatile gas to screen an escape and gag pursuers, an aerial cousin of the squid's ink. Alien versions might belch anything from acrid halogens to a cold fog of anesthetic vapor.

traits.name: core:reflex_bleeding
traits.description: The animal ruptures its own vessels to squirt foul, toxin-laden blood at attackers, as a horned lizard fires blood from its eyes or a ladybird oozes bitter hemolymph from its knees. Startling, disgusting, and often poisonous.

traits.name: core:bioluminescent_startle_flash
traits.description: A sudden burst of cold living light flashed to dazzle and startle a predator in the dark, as some brittle stars, click beetles, and deep-sea shrimp do. The blaze buys a heartbeat of confusion, just enough to vanish.

traits.name: core:luminous_sacrificial_slime
traits.description: The creature sheds a cloud or gob of glowing slime that sticks to and marks an attacker, painting it as a beacon for larger predators, as some deep-sea sea cucumbers and scale worms do. The victim flees still glowing, now hunted by the dark.

traits.name: core:electric_discharge_organ
traits.description: Stacked columns of modified muscle act as living batteries, delivering a stunning jolt to repel or kill, as an electric eel's 600-volt shock does. The same organ can double as radar and weapon.

traits.name: core:batesian_mimicry
traits.description: A harmless animal counterfeits the warning colors of a dangerous one, wearing a borrowed threat it cannot back up, as a hoverfly apes a wasp. The bluff works only while the truly dangerous model stays common enough to keep predators wary.

traits.name: core:mullerian_mimicry
traits.description: Several genuinely defended species converge on one shared warning pattern, so predators learn the single lesson faster and every member pays less in lost lives, as Heliconius butterflies do. Honest signals pooled into a common brand of danger.

traits.name: core:startle_eyespots
traits.description: Hidden patches that flash open into huge staring eyes, making a small animal seem to be a far larger predator's face, as peacock butterflies and some caterpillars do. The half-second of hesitation is often all the escape needed.

traits.name: core:flash_concealment_coloration
traits.description: Vivid hidden colors exposed in a startling flash while fleeing, then snapped shut so the animal seemingly vanishes the moment it stops, as in some grasshoppers and frogs. The pursuer chases an afterimage of color that no longer exists.

traits.name: core:limb_autotomy
traits.description: A built-in breakaway plane lets the animal jettison a limb or tail on demand, leaving a still-writhing piece to distract a predator while it escapes, as lizards and crabs do. The sacrifice is deliberate and the loss survivable.

traits.name: core:defensive_regeneration
traits.description: The power to regrow lost body parts, limbs, tails, even organs, from the wound outward, as a salamander or a starfish does. It turns a crippling injury into a temporary inconvenience and makes autotomy a viable gamble.

traits.name: core:autotomy_toxin_bait
traits.description: The discarded body part is not merely a wriggling distraction but a poison pill, packed with toxins and often glowing or thrashing, so the predator that seizes the bait is punished for taking it. An escape and a lesson delivered in one shed limb.

traits.name: core:antifreeze_dermal_glycoprotein
traits.description: The skin and body fluids are steeped in antifreeze glycoproteins that latch onto ice crystals and stop them from growing, letting an Antarctic icefish or a frozen wood frog shrug off temperatures that would shatter ordinary flesh. Armor not against teeth, but against the killing cold.

traits.name: core:cryptobiotic_encystment
traits.description: In a killing drought or freeze the animal expels its water, seals itself in a tough waxy cyst, and shrivels into a dormant tun, suspending life until conditions relent, exactly as a tardigrade does. In this armored stasis it can survive boiling, freezing, and the vacuum of space.

traits.name: core:uv_shield_pigment
traits.description: Dense screening pigments and mineral granules in the skin soak up ultraviolet light before it can damage tissue, a living sunscreen like the melanin of dark skin or the mycosporines of reef creatures. On a world with a thin ozone layer, it can be the difference between life and a lethal sunburn.

traits.name: core:sloughing_escape_skin
traits.description: Patches of skin or scales tear away cleanly the instant a predator grabs hold, leaving the attacker with a mouthful of shed integument while the animal slips free, as some geckos and skinks do. The bare patch heals over scar-free within weeks.

traits.name: core:mucus_sleep_cocoon
traits.description: At rest the animal spins a sealed envelope of mucus around itself, a scent-masking sleeping bag that hides its smell from night hunters, as a parrotfish does each evening. The bubble also filters out gill parasites while it sleeps.

traits.name: core:defensive_foam_froth
traits.description: The animal whips its secretions into a mass of bitter, sticky foam that shrouds its body and clogs a predator's mouthparts, as grasshoppers and froghoppers do. The froth can carry toxins and hisses out with an unnerving sputter.

traits.name: core:venomous_spur
traits.description: A sharp keratin spur near a limb, fed by a venom gland, used to slash and envenom rivals and attackers, as a male platypus does with a sting agonizing enough to fell a dog. Rare among backboned animals and unusually cruel.

traits.name: core:inflatable_body_bluff
traits.description: The animal gulps water or air to swell to several times its size, becoming too big to swallow and, in a pufferfish, a spiny sphere of menace. A meek target balloons into an impossible mouthful in seconds.

traits.name: core:erectile_threat_frill
traits.description: A collar or crest of skin that snaps open into a huge, often vividly colored fan, making the animal look suddenly enormous and fierce, as a frilled lizard does before it bolts. Bluff and ballast for a desperate run.

traits.name: core:alarm_pheromone_bleed
traits.description: Injured skin releases an alarm chemical that sends every neighbor of its kind fleeing for cover, the fright substance of shoaling minnows. A single wound becomes a warning broadcast to the whole school.

traits.name: core:reactive_crystallizing_slime
traits.description: A watery secretion that flash-hardens into a shell of interlocking mineral crystals the instant it meets air or an attacker's touch, sheathing the body in fresh armor on demand. No Earth creature armors itself this fast; the crust sloughs and regrows with each new threat.

traits.name: core:acid_sweat_dissolution
traits.description: Glands weep a strong acid across the whole body surface, so anything that grips the creature is chemically burned into letting go, a full-body version of the bombardier's spray. On some worlds the runoff etches the very rock the animal rests on.

traits.name: core:phosphor_glow_hemolymph
traits.description: The creature's blood glows with a cold, poisonous light, so a wound both smears the attacker with luminous marks and doses it with toxin. Bleeding becomes a double punishment, a beacon and a poison in one.

traits.name: core:electrothermal_burst
traits.description: Specialized organs dump a surge of current into a fluid jacket that flashes to scalding steam, firing a searing pulse of heat and shock at anything that draws near. Nothing on Earth wields a thermal-electric weapon; the creature can burn a hunter it never touches.

traits.name: core:spore_bomb_dispersal
traits.description: Pressurized structures rupture to blast a stinging cloud of spores or irritant dust at anything that disturbs them, as a puffball bursts underfoot. The cloud both defends the body and scatters the next generation.

traits.name: core:phytotoxin_laticifers
traits.description: A network of canals beneath the surface holds pressurized, toxic latex or resin that floods any wound, gumming up chewing mouthparts and poisoning the browser, as milkweeds and rubber trees do. Bite the plant and it weeps a sticky, bitter poison into the wound.

traits.name: core:silica_phytolith_armor
traits.description: The plant stiffens its tissues with countless microscopic grains of glassy silica, wearing down the teeth of grazers and slicing the guts of insects, the quiet arms race grasses wage against everything that eats them. A meadow is secretly an abrasive.

traits.name: core:hexagonal_dermal_mail
traits.description: The skin grows a perfect tessellation of interlocking hexagonal plates that flex like chainmail yet cover every angle without a gap, a geometric armor no Earth animal achieves so cleanly. Each plate can be shed and regrown, so the mail is always seamless.

traits.name: core:eyespot_phototaxis
traits.description: A pigmented cup shades a single photoreceptor, letting the organism read the direction of light and swim toward or away from it, as the green alga Euglena does with its orange stigma. The first, faintest ancestor of every eye.

traits.name: core:whole_body_photoreception
traits.description: Photoreceptors scattered across the entire skin turn the whole animal into a diffuse eye, sensing shadow and brightness without any focusing organ, the way a sea urchin uses its spine-studded test to 'see' looming predators. No image, just an all-over awareness of light and dark.

traits.name: core:motion_detection_ocelli
traits.description: Clusters of simple eyespots wired to a nerve net register nothing but sudden change, triggering a startle or flight response to a passing shadow. Like a box jellyfish's rhopalia, they answer only one question: did something just move.

traits.name: core:compound_eye
traits.description: Hundreds to thousands of ommatidia tile a domed lens into a mosaic image with a nearly panoramic field and blistering flicker-fusion speed, as in a dragonfly that tracks prey mid-flight. Superb at motion, coarse at detail; an alternative path to sight that forecloses the single-lens camera eye.

traits.name: core:stereoscopic_depth_vision
traits.description: Two forward-facing camera eyes with overlapping fields let a brain fuse the parallax into true depth, the way a mantis or a hawk ranges a strike to the millimeter. Binocular vision is the geometry of the ambush.

traits.name: core:uv_vision
traits.description: A photoreceptor tuned below 400nm opens a hidden channel of the world: flower nectar guides, urine trails, and plumage patterns that blaze in UV, as birds, bees, and reindeer see them. What looks blank to a human eye is a billboard to a UV eye.

traits.name: core:multispectral_vision
traits.description: A dozen or more photoreceptor classes span deep UV to far red, letting the eye read color as a spectrum scanned line by line rather than blended, as the mantis shrimp's sixteen-channel retina does. Baroque, metabolically ruinous, and almost unheard of.

traits.name: core:tetrachromacy
traits.description: A fourth cone type splits the visible spectrum into finer bands, letting the eye discriminate hundreds of hues where three cones blur them together, as many birds and reptiles do. The difference between a rainbow and a richer rainbow.

traits.name: core:tapetum_night_vision
traits.description: A mirrored layer behind the retina bounces unabsorbed photons back for a second pass, doubling sensitivity and lighting the eyes to an eerie shine in the dark, as a cat's or a crocodile's do. It buys sight in near-darkness at the cost of a little blur.

traits.name: core:mirror_eye
traits.description: Instead of a lens, a parabolic mirror of guanine crystals focuses light onto the retina, an optical trick a scallop packs into dozens of tiny blue eyes along its mantle. Rare, delicate, and utterly unlike the camera eye it competes with.

traits.name: core:scanning_retina
traits.description: A narrow high-resolution retina is swept mechanically across the scene like a searchlight, trading a fixed wide view for acute vision on a moving strip, as a jumping spider's principal eyes or a copepod's do. The eye itself flicks to read the world.

traits.name: core:parietal_third_eye
traits.description: A light-sensitive organ set into the top of the skull tracks day length, sun position, and overhead shadow without forming an image, as the tuatara's parietal eye and many lizards' do. A dorsal window for the clock and the hawk-shadow.

traits.name: core:panoramic_vision
traits.description: Eyes placed on the sides of the head sweep a nearly 360-degree field, so almost nothing can approach unseen, as a woodcock or a rabbit surveys the sky and horizon at once. Safety bought by trading depth for coverage.

traits.name: core:multifocal_pupil
traits.description: A stepped or U-shaped pupil splits incoming light into concentric zones focused separately, correcting color fringing and keeping several wavelengths sharp at once, as a cat's slit and some geckos' pupils do. Optics folded into the aperture itself.

traits.name: core:infrared_pit_sensing
traits.description: A membrane stretched across a facial pit acts as a thermal pinhole camera, mapping the body heat of warm prey onto a nerve sheet so the animal 'sees' warmth in total darkness, as a pit viper or a vampire bat does. A second, thermal image fused with sight.

traits.name: core:heat_bloom_imaging
traits.description: An array of pit organs is fused in the brain into a true infrared picture with edges, motion, and depth, letting a predator hunt purely by the glowing heat-shapes of living things. An alien way to render a warm world in the pitch dark.

traits.name: core:polarization_sky_compass
traits.description: By reading the great arc of polarized light the atmosphere scatters around the sun, the animal fixes a heading even under cloud, as a desert ant navigates a featureless dune. The whole sky becomes a compass rose only a polarized eye can see.

traits.name: core:tactile_vibrissae
traits.description: Stiff whiskers rooted in dense follicles of nerve endings feel the faintest brush and airflow, letting an animal map dark burrows and gauge gaps by touch alone, as a rat or a seal does. Active feelers that read the world by sweeping it.

traits.name: core:lateral_line_flow_sense
traits.description: A line of gel-capped hair cells running the body's flank feels the pressure waves and micro-currents of moving water, letting a fish school in the dark and sense a predator's bow wave, as every fish and many amphibians do. Distributed touch-at-a-distance in a fluid world.

traits.name: core:hydrodynamic_wake_tracking
traits.description: Ultra-sensitive whiskers read the swirling vortex trail a swimming animal leaves behind and follow it like a scent hanging in the water, as a harbor seal trails a fish it never sees. A hunt run entirely on invisible turbulence.

traits.name: core:tympanic_hearing
traits.description: A tensioned eardrum converts airborne pressure waves into vibrations a sensory organ can decode into pitch and rhythm, opening the entire acoustic world of calls, footfalls, and song, as insects and land vertebrates independently evolved. The membrane that lets air speak.

traits.name: core:ultrasound_sense
traits.description: Hearing extended far above the ordinary ceiling picks up the high shriek of echolocating hunters and the ultrasonic courtship of insects, letting a moth dive the instant a bat pings it. A private high-frequency channel above the audible world.

traits.name: core:infrasound_sense
traits.description: Hearing reaching below the human floor detects the deep rumble of distant storms, earthquakes, and the ground-borne calls of far-off herds, as elephants converse across kilometers and pigeons hear coming weather. The long, slow voice of the planet.

traits.name: core:acoustic_parallax_localization
traits.description: Paired ears and a brain circuit that clocks sub-millisecond arrival differences pin a sound to a precise point in space, letting a barn owl strike a mouse by its rustle in total darkness. Hearing sharpened into a targeting system.

traits.name: core:seismic_tremorsense
traits.description: Specialized receptors in the feet or body read vibrations traveling through the ground, letting an animal feel footfalls, digging, and distant thunder through the soil, as elephants detect herds through their soles and scorpions locate prey by substrate ripples. The earth itself becomes an ear.

traits.name: core:substrate_vibration_sense
traits.description: Simple mechanoreceptors pressed to leaf, web, or stem feel the tremor of an approaching mate or meal traveling through the surface, as a spider reads its web and a treehopper drums on a stalk. The most ancient way to feel the unseen.

traits.name: core:seismic_communication
traits.description: Animals deliberately drum, stomp, or shake to send coded vibrations through the ground to distant kin, a private channel no airborne eavesdropper can hear, as elephants and some frogs and spiders do. Conversation carried by the earth.

traits.name: core:statocyst_gravity_sense
traits.description: A tiny grain of dense mineral rolls inside a hair-lined sac, and wherever it presses tells the animal which way is down, as jellyfish and crustaceans keep themselves upright. The simplest possible sense of gravity, older than any brain.

traits.name: core:vestibular_balance_sense
traits.description: Fluid-filled loops set at right angles detect head rotation in every plane while gravity-sensing patches track tilt, giving a fast, precise sense of balance and acceleration, as the inner ear does in vertebrates. The gyroscope that keeps a runner or a flyer from tumbling.

traits.name: core:proprioception
traits.description: Stretch receptors woven through muscle and joint report the exact posture of every limb without sight, letting an animal walk in the dark and place a foot precisely, as vertebrate muscle spindles do. The silent, constant sense of one's own body in space.

traits.name: core:barometric_pressure_sense
traits.description: Delicate receptors register slow shifts in air pressure, warning of incoming storms and coaxing insects to feed or mate before the front hits, as birds and honeybees seem to anticipate weather. A barometer wired into behavior.

traits.name: core:hydrostatic_depth_sense
traits.description: By reading water pressure against a gas bladder or specialized organ, an aquatic animal knows its exact depth and holds a chosen layer of the water column, as fish tune buoyancy against the crushing gradient of the deep. A sense calibrated in fathoms.

traits.name: core:distributed_chemoreception
traits.description: Taste and smell receptors spread across the whole skin turn the entire body surface into a tongue, letting an animal taste its surroundings from every angle, as a catfish is a swimming taste bud carpeted in over a hundred thousand receptors. To touch is to taste.

traits.name: core:vomeronasal_pheromone_sense
traits.description: A dedicated organ in the roof of the mouth samples heavy, non-volatile chemical signals, reading reproductive state, territory, and identity from a lick or a flehmen curl, as snakes and cats do. A second nose devoted entirely to the chemistry of other animals.

traits.name: core:pheromone_plume_tracking
traits.description: Feathery antennae so sensitive they can register a handful of scent molecules let an animal fly upwind along a mate's drifting plume for kilometers, as a silk moth traces a single female across the night. Navigation by a thread of smell.

traits.name: core:hygroreception
traits.description: Humidity-sensing sensilla let an animal feel the moisture of the air and steer toward damp refuge or away from killing dryness, as cockroaches and beetles find water they cannot see. A sense tuned to the thirst of the world.

traits.name: core:thermoreception
traits.description: Temperature-gated ion channels in the skin report warmth and cold directly, letting an animal seek a sunny stone or flee a scorching surface, the same TRP receptors that make chili burn and mint feel cool. The baseline sense of hot and cold.

traits.name: core:cryoreception
traits.description: Dedicated cold-tuned receptors give fine resolution deep into freezing temperatures, letting a polar or high-altitude animal find the one survivable microclimate and avoid frostbite, an acuity honed where cold itself is the predator. A thermometer specialized for the killing end of the scale.

traits.name: core:nociception
traits.description: High-threshold receptors fire only at damaging stimuli, translating injury into the urgent signal of pain that yanks the body away from harm and teaches it to avoid the cause, a sense shared from flatworms to mammals. The alarm that protects the flesh.

traits.name: core:passive_electroreception
traits.description: Jelly-filled pores tuned to faint voltages detect the electric fields every living muscle leaks into the water, letting a shark find a fish buried in sand or a platypus hunt with its eyes shut. The heartbeat of prey, read straight off the water.

traits.name: core:active_electrolocation
traits.description: The animal generates its own electric field and reads the distortions objects cast in it, building a picture of its surroundings in muddy, lightless water, as elephantnose and knifefish do. A self-illuminated sense that paints the dark with electricity.

traits.name: core:electric_image_perception
traits.description: A dedicated brain region fuses the electric-organ discharge and its echoes into a true three-dimensional electric picture, resolving shape, distance, and even the conductivity of hidden prey. Seeing with electricity as vividly as another animal sees with light.

traits.name: core:electrocommunication
traits.description: Animals modulate the frequency and pattern of their electric discharges into signals of identity, rank, and courtship, chatting on a channel only other electric fish can hear, and politely shifting frequencies to avoid jamming a neighbor. Society conducted in voltage.

traits.name: core:electrostatic_field_sense
traits.description: Fine sensory hairs deflect in the ambient atmospheric electric field, letting an animal feel the charge of the air itself: a bee reads a flower's electrostatic halo to know if it was just visited, and a spider senses the field it will ride to balloon aloft. Sensing electricity in dry air, where no ampulla could work.

traits.name: core:magnetite_compass
traits.description: Chains of biologically grown magnetite crystals swing to align with the planet's magnetic field, giving even a single cell a fixed sense of north, as magnetotactic bacteria orient along field lines to find their preferred depth. The simplest magnet a body can build.

traits.name: core:quantum_magnetoreception
traits.description: Light striking a cryptochrome protein spins up a pair of entangled radical electrons whose chemistry bends with the planet's magnetic field, letting the animal literally see its heading as a pattern laid over vision, as a migrating robin appears to do. A compass that runs on quantum spin and dies in the dark.

traits.name: core:magnetic_map_sense
traits.description: By reading both the inclination and intensity of the geomagnetic field, the brain fixes not just a heading but a position, an innate global grid that guides a hatchling sea turtle across an ocean to a beach it has never seen. A map printed in magnetism and inherited whole.

traits.name: core:em_field_sense
traits.description: A lattice of resonant organic antennae feels oscillating electromagnetic fields across a wide band, so the buzz of a power line, the flicker of another creature's nerves, and the sky's electromagnetic weather all register as a felt texture. An alien sense with no clean Earth analog, sprawling far past any ampulla.

traits.name: core:radio_reception
traits.description: Coiled conductive filaments act as a living antenna tuned to radio wavelengths, so distant lightning, stellar static, and even the pulse of a technological world arrive as a low murmur in the mind. Almost impossible to evolve, and staggering where it does.

traits.name: core:magnetospheric_sense
traits.description: Beyond a mere compass, this sense feels the living turbulence of the planet's magnetosphere, the daily breathing of field lines and the shudder of solar storms, letting the creature brace before an aurora blooms. It reads the sky's magnetic weather like a coming tide.

traits.name: core:atmospheric_ion_sense
traits.description: Receptors count the charged particles suspended in the air, and the rising tang of ions before a thunderstorm or beneath a charged sky becomes a vivid warning to shelter. A sense that tastes the electric mood of the atmosphere itself.

traits.name: core:plasma_flux_sense
traits.description: On worlds bathed in raw stellar wind, an organ feels the streaming flux of charged plasma against the magnetosphere, so the creature knows when the star has flared and the sky above is about to burn. A sense for the weather of space, unimaginable on a sheltered world.

traits.name: core:radiation_dosimetry_sense
traits.description: Melanin-rich tissue or radiation-triggered receptors register the sleet of ionizing radiation directly, so a creature on an irradiated world feels a hot zone as surely as a mammal feels heat and crawls away from a lethal glow. A Geiger counter grown from flesh, born where radiation is the great danger.

traits.name: core:gravitational_gradient_sense
traits.description: A body strung with exquisitely dense sensor masses feels the minute differences in gravity between one part of itself and another, reading the pull of mountains, the void of a chasm, or a heavy mass hidden underground. A sense that maps the world by weight alone, wildly improbable and almost magical.

traits.name: core:tidal_gravitation_sense
traits.description: Fine gravity receptors track the slow lunar and solar pull that raises the tides, letting an animal time spawning to a precise phase of the tidal cycle, as grunion ride the highest wave to breed on the sand. A clock set by the tug of distant worlds.

traits.name: core:coriolis_rotation_sense
traits.description: Ultra-stable vestibular loops feel the faint, ceaseless deflection of the planet's own spin, giving the creature an absolute sense of which way the world turns and, from it, latitude itself. An inertial sense so refined it perceives the rotation of the whole globe.

traits.name: core:spin_state_sense
traits.description: Extending the radical-pair trick to its limit, arrays of coupled spins let the creature read quantum states in its surroundings and perhaps sense correlations at a distance, a faculty perched at the far edge of what chemistry can do. A sense that reaches into the quantum substrate of matter, almost never realized.

traits.name: core:neutrino_ghost_sense
traits.description: A vast crystalline organ, grown over deep evolutionary time, registers the ghostly passage of neutrinos that stream unhindered through a whole planet, so the creature feels the core of its star and the flash of a distant supernova as a faint interior shiver. The most improbable sense imaginable, a body somehow catching the untouchable.

traits.name: core:standing_wave_pressure_sense
traits.description: On a world of dense fluid or thick atmosphere, an organ tuned to resonate with standing pressure waves reads the interference patterns bouncing between distant surfaces, mapping the shape of a whole basin from the hum it holds. An alien echolocation that listens to the environment's own resonance rather than a call.

traits.name: core:size_nano
traits.description: Sub-micron bodies smaller than most bacteria, like the ultramicroscopic candidate 'nanobes' or the parasite Nanoarchaeum clinging to a larger host. Life pared to the thermodynamic floor, where a cell barely has room for its own ribosomes.

traits.name: core:size_titan
traits.description: Bodies beyond the mega grade, hundreds of tonnes on land or kilometre-spanning stands, pushing the structural limits of any known tissue. Picture a sauropod outgrowing the reach of its own heart, or a honey-fungus mat sprawling across a whole forest.

traits.name: core:buoyant_gigantism
traits.description: Water's lift lets a body balloon past anything limbs could carry, like a blue whale whose own weight would crush it on land. Mass becomes a solved problem once the medium holds you up.

traits.name: core:allometric_scaling
traits.description: Proportions shift with size, so limbs thicken faster than bodies lengthen and a scaled-up animal is a re-engineered one, not a mere enlargement. An elephant's pillar legs are a different machine from a shrew's twigs.

traits.name: core:isometric_scaling
traits.description: Growth that keeps the same shape at every size, each stage a faithful enlargement of the last. It works only across a narrow span before physics forces a redesign of the body plan.

traits.name: core:island_dwarfism
traits.description: Isolation on small ranges with scarce food shrinks big lineages generation by generation, as the dwarf elephants of Mediterranean islands and the hobbit-sized Homo floresiensis attest.

traits.name: core:island_gigantism
traits.description: Freed from large predators, small island colonists swell into giants, like Komodo dragons, giant tortoises, and the dodo's oversized pigeon frame.

traits.name: core:indeterminate_growth
traits.description: The body never stops growing while it lives, so age and size climb together. Lobsters, sturgeon, and many reptiles keep enlarging until something else finally kills them.

traits.name: core:determinate_growth
traits.description: Growth halts at a genetically fixed adult size, freeing energy from expansion for reproduction and precise body engineering, as in birds, mammals, and insects.

traits.name: core:modular_indeterminate_growth
traits.description: The body is an open-ended series of repeated modules, leaves or polyps or fronds, added without limit, so a coral head or a tree has no final form, only more of itself.

traits.name: core:high_gravity_dwarfing
traits.description: On a heavy world every gram is a burden, so bodies stay squat and small to keep from collapsing under their own weight, a planetary tax on bigness written into the skeleton.

traits.name: core:low_gravity_gigantism
traits.description: Where gravity is gentle, bodies stretch tall and vast on gossamer supports a heavier world would snap. Spindly giants that could never stand upright on Earth.

traits.name: core:seasonal_size_shift
traits.description: The body physically shrinks and regrows with the seasons, even the braincase and organs, to cut winter upkeep, exactly as shrews and moles do in Dehnel's phenomenon.

traits.name: core:phenotypic_size_plasticity
traits.description: Final size is set by circumstance, not just genes, so the same larva becomes a giant on rich food or a runt on poor. One genome hedged across good years and bad.

traits.name: core:gigantothermy
traits.description: Sheer bulk holds heat: a body so large it barely cools, staying warm through thermal inertia alone without burning fuel to do it, as in leatherback turtles and, likely, the great dinosaurs.

traits.name: core:polyploid_gigantism
traits.description: Extra whole-genome copies swell every cell, and a body of bigger cells grows bigger overall, the trick behind giant salamanders' vast genomes and many oversized plants.

traits.name: core:hypertrophic_cell_gigantism
traits.description: The body grows not by adding cells but by inflating the ones it has, like a fly's giant salivary cells with their banded polytene chromosomes. Fewer, enormous units instead of many small ones.

traits.name: core:extreme_miniaturization
traits.description: A full body plan crammed into a near-microscopic frame, organs simplified or lost to make it fit. The frog Paedophryne at seven millimetres, or myxozoans reduced from jellyfish to a handful of cells.

traits.name: core:extreme_size_dimorphism
traits.description: The sexes diverge wildly in size: a blanket octopus female tens of thousands of times her mate's mass, or an anglerfish male fused to her flank as a parasitic speck.

traits.name: core:giant_unicell
traits.description: One cell grown to macroscopic size: the seaweed Caulerpa spans a metre as a single cell, and Valonia bubbles up like a green marble. A lone membrane holding a whole organism's worth of cytoplasm.

traits.name: core:agglutinated_test_giant
traits.description: A single deep-sea amoeba centimetres wide that cements sediment grains into an ornate shell around itself. The xenophyophore, the largest single cell known, a giant built out of borrowed mud.

traits.name: core:coenocytic_body
traits.description: Nuclei divide again and again but the cell itself never does, yielding one continuous cytoplasm threaded with thousands of nuclei, the architecture of Caulerpa and many molds.

traits.name: core:syncytial_giant
traits.description: Many cells fuse, dissolving their walls into one vast multinucleate mass that acts as a single body. Glass sponges wire themselves this way, and it could scale up to an entire alien creature sharing one cytoplasm.

traits.name: core:plasmodial_network
traits.description: A single crawling sheet of streaming cytoplasm with millions of nuclei, like the slime mold Physarum flowing over the forest floor and pulsing to shuttle food through its body.

traits.name: core:acellular_path_network
traits.description: The plasmodium computes: it thins unused strands and thickens efficient routes until its body maps the shortest paths between food, famously re-drawing the Tokyo rail network in a dish.

traits.name: core:biofilm
traits.description: Cells embed themselves in a shared slime matrix, sticking to surfaces and to each other, as in dental plaque, pond scum, and the slick on a river stone. The simplest step from lone cell toward community.

traits.name: core:microbial_mat
traits.description: Biofilms stacked into layered sheets where each stratum runs a different chemistry, from sunlit photosynthesizers on top to sulfur-eaters in the dark below. The oldest ecosystems on any world.

traits.name: core:stromatolite_builder
traits.description: A mat that traps and cements mineral grains layer by layer, growing into domed rock reefs over millennia. Cyanobacterial stromatolites ruled the seas for two billion years.

traits.name: core:quorum_sensing
traits.description: Cells release and count chemical signals, holding an action until enough neighbors agree, the vote by which bacteria light up in unison or launch an attack only once they are a crowd.

traits.name: core:facultative_aggregation
traits.description: Solitary cells that stream together into a single body only when times turn hard, then part ways again, like the social amoeba Dictyostelium gathering when the food runs out.

traits.name: core:migratory_slug
traits.description: Tens of thousands of gathered amoebae crawl as one slug toward light and warmth, then some sacrifice themselves into a dead stalk to hoist the rest skyward as spores. A body built and dissolved on demand.

traits.name: core:zooid_colony
traits.description: Many genetically identical bodies bud and stay joined into one physical colony, like a coral head or a lace of bryozoans, each unit a zooid contributing to the whole.

traits.name: core:siphonophore_modular
traits.description: A colony whose clonal zooids specialize so completely they become organs, one for swimming, one for stinging, one for digestion, so that a Portuguese man o' war is not an animal but a fleet mistaken for one.

traits.name: core:zooid_polymorphism
traits.description: The colony casts its members into distinct forms for distinct jobs: feeding autozooids, and defensive avicularia that snap shut like tiny jaws. Division of labor written straight into anatomy.

traits.name: core:obligate_coloniality
traits.description: The colony can no longer be undone: a lone zooid cannot survive, so the collective becomes the individual, its parts as dependent on one another as the cells of any single body.

traits.name: core:superorganism
traits.description: Separate mobile bodies integrate so tightly that the group thinks and acts as one: an ant colony as a single distributed animal, its workers the cells and its queen the germ line.

traits.name: core:clonal_ramet_network
traits.description: One genet spreads as a web of rooted clones sharing runners or rhizomes, like the aspen grove Pando, a single tree with forty thousand trunks, or a seagrass meadow that is all one plant.

traits.name: core:clonal_integration
traits.description: Connected clones pool and share resources, so ramets stuck in shade or drought are fed by siblings in sun and water. A single organism hedging its bets across a patchy world.

traits.name: core:genet_immortality
traits.description: Individual ramets die but the genet never does, renewing itself clonally for tens of thousands of years, a body that sidesteps aging by continually replacing its own parts.

traits.name: core:budding_propagation
traits.description: New individuals grow directly off the parent's body and either drop free or stay attached, as hydra bud from their flanks and yeast pinch off daughters. The simplest engine of colony growth.

traits.name: core:coenobium_colony
traits.description: A colony of a fixed, species-typical number of cells set in a defined geometry, like the flat plates of Gonium or the sixteen-cell tablets of Pandorina. Coloniality built to a blueprint.

traits.name: core:volvocine_grade
traits.description: A hollow rolling sphere of cells that first splits labor between a mortal swimming soma and an immortal reproductive germ. Volvox, a living model of how bodies first set aside a germ line.

traits.name: core:soma_germ_separation
traits.description: The lineage divides its cells into a disposable body and a protected reproductive line, the founding bargain of complex multicellularity, where most cells give up their own future to serve the few that carry it forward.

traits.name: core:filamentous_multicellularity
traits.description: Cells stay joined end to end after dividing, forming unbranched or branching threads, as in cyanobacterial trichomes and algal filaments. The cheapest route to a body with a top and a bottom.

traits.name: core:heterocyst_differentiation
traits.description: Along a filament, occasional cells specialize into thick-walled nitrogen-fixers that shield their oxygen-hating enzymes for the whole chain, Anabaena's first true division of labor between cells.

traits.name: core:colonial_photosymbiosis
traits.description: A colony farms photosynthetic tenants inside its own tissues, trading shelter for sugar, like reef corals glowing with zooxanthellae or giant clams gardening light in their mantles.

traits.name: core:reef_framework_engineer
traits.description: Colonies lay down mineral skeleton generation upon generation until they have built the very reef they live on, engineering habitat for a whole ecosystem, as corals, rudist clams, and ancient sponge bioherms did.

traits.name: core:colonial_calcification
traits.description: The whole colony secretes a shared mineral fortress, its individual cups fused into one continuous skeleton that outlives its builders, the stony architecture of a coral head.

traits.name: core:aggregative_multicellularity
traits.description: A body assembled from unrelated cells that come together rather than staying joined after division, a route to multicellularity riddled with the risk of cheaters, taken by slime molds and myxobacteria.

traits.name: core:clonal_multicellularity
traits.description: A body built the reliable way, with cells staying together after dividing so that every cell is kin and cooperation comes cheap. The path that led to plants, animals, and fungi.

traits.name: core:distributed_multibody
traits.description: A single organism partitioned across many independently mobile bodies that share one genome and one purpose, coordinating with no center at all. Imagine a creature that is a scattered flock yet grieves as one when a body is lost.

traits.name: core:fractal_selfsimilar_colony
traits.description: The colony repeats its own shape at every scale, sub-colonies nested within colonies within super-colonies, each tier a smaller echo of the whole. A living Mandelbrot that divides labor across orders of magnitude.

traits.name: core:seasonal_aggregate_superbody
traits.description: Solitary for most of the year, the creatures fuse each season into one enormous shared body, pooling flesh, warmth, and senses through the lean months, then dissolve back into wandering individuals when plenty returns.

traits.name: core:swarm_body
traits.description: A cloud of small bodies that behaves as a single organism, flowing, splitting, and reforming around obstacles and digesting prey it engulfs together. A body with no fixed shape, only a shared rulebook.

traits.name: core:hive_gestalt
traits.description: Minds networked across bodies into one distributed intelligence, thinking thoughts no single body could hold. A collective consciousness where the boundary of 'self' is drawn around the whole colony.

traits.name: core:fissioning_supraorganism
traits.description: When it grows too large the whole colony splits like a dividing cell, budding off a daughter super-organism that marches away. Reproduction at the scale of societies, as army-ant colonies swarm and divide.

traits.name: core:mobile_biofilm_raft
traits.description: A microbial mat that tears free and swims, its cells beating in unison to propel the whole sheet like a living carpet crossing open water in search of fresh light and food.

traits.name: core:tessellated_sheet_colony
traits.description: Identical units lock edge to edge into a flawless living tiling, a flexible pavement of clones that grows only at its rim, like a chain-mail sheet of interlocking cells draped across the seafloor.

traits.name: core:nested_symbiotic_consortium
traits.description: The 'individual' is a matryoshka of species, symbionts inside symbionts inside a host, all acting as one body, as the protist Mixotricha swims on bacteria it mistakes for its own cilia.

traits.name: core:cephalized_colony
traits.description: The colony grows a permanent leading pole that senses and steers for the whole, the 'front' where a siphonophore hangs its float and hunting tackle. The first hint of a head at the level of the collective.

traits.name: core:continuous_tissue_fusion
traits.description: Separate individuals meet and merge their tissues into a single shared body of mixed genomes, as colonial tunicates fuse when they touch, blurring exactly where one creature ends and another begins.

traits.name: core:multinucleate_hyphae
traits.description: A foraging web of tubular filaments with no cross-walls, nuclei and cytoplasm streaming freely through the whole network, the body plan of many molds and of the vast underground mycelia of fungi.

traits.name: core:heliothermic_basking
traits.description: Like a lizard flattened broadside on a morning rock, orienting to the sun and shuttling between shade and light to hold its blood at working temperature without burning a single calorie of its own.

traits.name: core:regional_heterothermy
traits.description: The body abandons a single uniform temperature, running a scalding core wrapped in near-freezing limbs — like a reindeer's leg held just above frostbite or a tuna's cruising muscle kept far warmer than the sea.

traits.name: core:countercurrent_heat_exchange
traits.description: Interwoven arteries and veins trade warmth before it can escape — the rete mirabile of a tuna's flank or a goose's foot, where outgoing blood pre-heats the cold blood returning so precious heat never reaches the skin to be lost.

traits.name: core:hibernation
traits.description: Like a ground squirrel letting its heart fall to a few beats a minute and its body drift toward freezing, spending the barren season as a barely-living ember and rewarming, weeks later, from the inside out.

traits.name: core:daily_torpor
traits.description: Like a hummingbird every cold night, collapsing into a nightly coma to keep from starving before dawn, then shivering its furnace back to full blaze at first light.

traits.name: core:estivation
traits.description: Like a lungfish sealing itself in a cocoon of dried mucus in the cracking mud, or a snail cemented shut against the summer, waiting out heat and drought in a slowed hush until the rains return.

traits.name: core:brown_fat_thermogenesis
traits.description: A special fat that burns fuel purely to make heat, its cellular furnaces deliberately uncoupled from useful work — how a newborn mammal or a rewarming bat floods itself with warmth without a single shiver.

traits.name: core:shivering_thermogenesis
traits.description: Muscles fire against one another to make heat instead of motion — the universal mammalian shiver, and the pre-flight tremor of a bumblebee or hawk-moth buzzing its wings warm before it can lift off.

traits.name: core:evaporative_cooling
traits.description: Like a panting dog or a sweating horse, dumping heat by boiling off water across wet tissue — cheap and effective right up until the water runs out under a merciless sun.

traits.name: core:eccrine_sweating
traits.description: Skin studded with glands that weep water directly onto the body's surface to carry heat away as it evaporates — the near-hairless, sweat-drenched cooling that lets a human run a horse to exhaustion in the midday heat.

traits.name: core:gular_fluttering
traits.description: A bird with no sweat glands vibrates the thin floor of its throat dozens of times a second, fanning moist membranes to shed heat by evaporation — the throat-quiver of a nesting cormorant or a nighthawk panting in the shade.

traits.name: core:nasal_heat_recovery
traits.description: Scrolled bones in the snout cool the outgoing breath, condensing back its heat and water before it is lost — how a kangaroo rat exhales air colder and drier than it took in, and survives a whole desert on the water hidden in its seeds.

traits.name: core:facultative_endothermy
traits.description: A cold-blooded body that heats chosen organs on demand — the warmed eyes and brain of a swordfish, a mako's furnace of red muscle, or a brooding python shivering its coils to incubate a clutch.

traits.name: core:behavioral_thermoregulation
traits.description: Temperature managed entirely by choice of place and posture — burrowing at noon, climbing to the sun at dawn, pressing against warm stone or splaying into a breeze, the cheapest thermostat any animal owns.

traits.name: core:piloerection
traits.description: Tiny muscles stand the coat on end, puffing fur or feathers into a thicker cushion of trapped, still air — the fluffed-out sparrow doubling in size on a frozen morning, or the bristling of a chilled cat.

traits.name: core:terminator_physiology
traits.description: On a world with one face locked forever to its star, life clings to the ring of endless twilight — one flank baked, the other frozen — and plumbs heat sideways through its own body, drawing warmth from its dayside limbs to keep its nightside from freezing solid.

traits.name: core:thermogenic_bloom
traits.description: A rooted, sunlit organism that burns its own stored sugars purely to make heat, holding its flower more than twenty degrees above the freezing air — like an eastern skunk cabbage melting its way up through late snow and volatilizing scent to lure the season's first flies.

traits.name: core:gill_respiration
traits.description: Feathery, blood-rich membranes held out into the water to strip dissolved oxygen from it, ventilated by a one-way current — the branching red gills of a fish or the plumed tufts on a sea slug's back.

traits.name: core:book_lungs
traits.description: Stacked leaves of thin tissue folded like the pages of a book inside a body cavity, holding a huge breathing surface in a small, humid space — the ancient air organ of a spider or scorpion, drawing oxygen without ever drying out.

traits.name: core:tracheal_respiration
traits.description: Air piped straight to every cell through a branching network of tubes that open through valved pores in the body wall, delivering oxygen without any blood at all — the whistling tracheae of an insect, so efficient at small size yet so limiting that they cap how large the body can ever grow.

traits.name: core:flow_through_air_sacs
traits.description: Balloon-like sacs threaded through the body drive air one way through rigid lungs, so fresh oxygen washes the exchange surface on both the breath in and the breath out — the ultra-efficient flow-through system that lets a bird power flight over a mountaintop where a mammal would faint.

traits.name: core:cutaneous_respiration
traits.description: Breathing straight through moist, permeable skin, no lungs required — the entire strategy of a lungless salamander, and a frog's backup for the long cold months spent underwater beneath the ice.

traits.name: core:swim_bladder
traits.description: A gas-filled sac tuned to hold the body weightless at depth, its pressure trimmed breath by breath — evolved from a primitive lung, and in some fish still doubling as one, gulped full of air at the surface of a stagnant pool.

traits.name: core:plastron_respiration
traits.description: A permanent silver film of air held against the body by a dense pelt of water-repellent hairs, working as a physical gill that pulls fresh oxygen out of the surrounding water — so an aquatic beetle can stay submerged indefinitely without ever surfacing to breathe.

traits.name: core:labyrinth_organ
traits.description: A maze of folded, blood-rich bone above the gills that lets a fish gulp and breathe air directly, surviving in warm, foul, oxygen-starved water — the accessory lung of a betta or a walking catfish crossing wet ground between ponds.

traits.name: core:hemocyanin
traits.description: Copper rather than iron carries the oxygen, staining the blood blue and outperforming red blood in the cold, high-pressure dark — the pigment of an octopus, a horseshoe crab, and the deep-sea giants of the abyss.

traits.name: core:antifreeze_proteins
traits.description: Proteins that latch onto the first specks of ice and stop them growing, holding body fluids liquid well below their natural freezing point — the reason an Antarctic icefish swims, unfrozen, through water cold enough to kill it.

traits.name: core:supercooling
traits.description: The body purges every mote that ice could crystallize around and stays liquid far below freezing, a held breath of thermodynamic luck — like an overwintering beetle larva chilled to minus thirty and still, impossibly, unfrozen.

traits.name: core:freeze_tolerance
traits.description: The opposite gamble to supercooling: let the body freeze on purpose but govern exactly where, packing cells with sugars so ice forms only in the safe spaces between them — how a wood frog stops its heart, freezes to a solid lump, and thaws back to life in spring.

traits.name: core:ice_nucleating_proteins
traits.description: Proteins that deliberately seed ice in harmless places at a gentle, high temperature, so freezing creeps in slowly and outside the cells rather than striking as a sudden lethal shatter — the quiet trick behind surviving a body frozen solid.

traits.name: core:cryptobiosis
traits.description: Metabolism dialed flat to zero — no measurable life at all — the organism crumpling into a dormant tun that can wait out years, then swell and stir back to life with a single drop of water, exactly as a tardigrade does.

traits.name: core:anhydrobiosis
traits.description: Life without water: the body sheds nearly all its moisture and replaces it with protective sugars, drying to a brittle husk that can blow on the wind for decades and revive the moment it is wetted — the survival mode of a resurrection plant or a brine-shrimp egg.

traits.name: core:trehalose_vitrification
traits.description: As it dries, the body floods its cells with a sugar that sets into a stable glass, freezing every delicate molecule in place so nothing can unravel or drift — the molecular scaffolding that lets desiccated life survive intact for years.

traits.name: core:radiation_resistance
traits.description: A genome shattered into hundreds of pieces by radiation, then stitched back together flawlessly within hours — the almost unkillable repair machinery of Deinococcus, a microbe that shrugs off a thousand times the dose that would kill a human.

traits.name: core:radiotrophic_melanism
traits.description: Black with melanin, the organism turns ionizing radiation itself into usable energy, growing faster the hotter its surroundings glow — the strange dark fungi that thrive on the walls of a ruined reactor core.

traits.name: core:thermophily
traits.description: Proteins and membranes stiffened to hold their shape in scalding water that would cook ordinary life, thriving where a hot spring steams — perfectly comfortable at temperatures that would hard-boil an egg.

traits.name: core:oligotrophy
traits.description: Life tuned to near-starvation, harvesting the faintest traces of nutrient from clean rock or deep water and growing so slowly that a single generation may take a thousand years — the patient poverty of the deep subsurface.

traits.name: core:anaerobic_dormancy
traits.description: The ability to switch off the need for oxygen entirely and idle in the airless dark — like a carp under winter ice or a gut parasite, running a slow anaerobic economy for months where nothing else could breathe.

traits.name: core:heat_shock_proteins
traits.description: Molecular chaperones that rush to catch and refold proteins as heat begins to unravel them, an emergency crew thrown up whenever the body overheats — one of the oldest and most universal survival responses life has.

traits.name: core:metabolic_depression
traits.description: The deliberate dimming of the flame — dropping the body's whole metabolic rate to a small fraction of normal on command, the shared engine beneath hibernation, estivation, and every long dormancy, buying time when fuel or oxygen runs short.

traits.name: core:cold_hardening
traits.description: A hurried retooling in the hours before a freeze — flooding the body with glycerol and other antifreeze solutes and remodeling membranes to stay fluid — so a chill that would have been lethal yesterday is survived tonight.

traits.name: core:osmoconforming
traits.description: Rather than fight the surrounding saltiness, the body matches its own internal concentration to the water outside — the urea-loaded blood of a shark, or a hagfish as salty inside as the sea, spending nothing to hold a gradient that would otherwise bleed it dry.

traits.name: core:vacuum_tolerance
traits.description: Curled into a desiccated husk, the organism survives naked exposure to the vacuum and radiation of open space — boiled dry, frozen, and irradiated all at once, yet stirring back to life when returned to water, as a tardigrade has done in orbit.

traits.name: core:diving_apnea_reflex
traits.description: On the plunge, the heart crashes to a crawl and blood is shunted from skin and gut to brain and core, letting the body ride its oxygen stores in a slow, blue-cold hush — how a seal or a sperm whale hunts for an hour on a single breath.

traits.name: core:blubber_insulation
traits.description: A thick, oil-rich blanket of fat wrapped around the whole body, insulation that doubles as fuel and buoyancy — the warmth that lets a whale or a walrus stay hot-blooded in water cold enough to kill an unprotected swimmer in minutes.

traits.name: core:insulating_pelage
traits.description: A dense undercoat of fine hairs traps a still layer of warm air against the skin, so effective that snow settles unmelted on the animal's back — the deep winter pelt of a musk ox or an arctic fox curled against the wind.

traits.name: core:down_insulation
traits.description: Fluffy, branchless feathers with no aerodynamic job at all, tangling into a loft of trapped air beneath the outer coat — the down that keeps an eider duckling or a nestling warm on a freezing shore.

traits.name: core:thermal_melanism
traits.description: Darkened for heat rather than for hiding, a near-black body soaks up sunlight and warms far faster in the cold — the jet coloring of an alpine butterfly or a high-mountain lizard that must bask itself alive each frozen morning.

traits.name: core:huddling_thermoregulation
traits.description: Bodies pressed into a single shared mass, pooling their heat and taking turns on the frozen outside — the slow, rotating huddle of emperor penguins outlasting a polar winter, or a knot of bats clustered warm in a cave.

traits.name: core:pyrophilic_infrared_sensing
traits.description: Pit organs tuned to the infrared glow of a distant wildfire draw the animal toward the flames instead of away — the fire-beetle that flies miles to a fresh burn to lay its eggs in wood still too hot for any rival to touch.

traits.name: core:diapause
traits.description: Development slammed to a programmed halt, often sealed inside an egg, so the young simply skip a hostile season and resume only when the right cue arrives — the drought-proof eggs of a killifish waiting years in dried mud for the next flood.

traits.name: core:endolithic_dormancy
traits.description: Life retreated into the pore spaces inside solid rock, sheltering from killing cold, drought, and radiation behind a millimeter of translucent stone and creeping along on the faint light and moisture that seep in — the cryptic communities living inside Antarctic sandstone.

traits.name: core:brumation
traits.description: The cold-blooded winter shutdown — a reptile or amphibian sinking to the bottom of a pond or deep into a burrow, heart barely beating, sometimes breathing through its skin for months, waiting motionless for spring to warm it back to motion.

traits.name: core:phagotrophy
traits.description: The ancestral heterotroph trick: wrapping a false foot around a smaller particle and swallowing it whole into a food vacuole, as an amoeba engulfs a bacterium. Eating became a matter of reaching out and folding the world inward.

traits.name: core:cellular_predation
traits.description: A single cell hunts another single cell, piercing its victim's membrane and siphoning out the cytoplasm like a straw in a juice box — the way vampyrellid amoebae drill algae. Predator and prey are the same size, and the losing cell is simply drained.

traits.name: core:bacterivory
traits.description: A living filter that grazes the invisible: sweeping bacteria out of water or biofilm by the thousand, as a ciliate's oral groove does. The base of nearly every food web starts with something small enough to eat the smallest things.

traits.name: core:microbial_grazing
traits.description: Scraping the fuzz off the world — rasping mats of bacteria and algae from rock and reef with a toothed tongue, the way a snail's radula planes a film off glass. Wherever sunlight grows slime, something evolves to mow it.

traits.name: core:suspension_feeding
traits.description: Turning the whole animal into a sieve: pumping water through a mesh of cilia, gills, or mucus and combing out whatever drifts by, as a sponge or a mussel does silently for its entire life. Food is not hunted but strained from the current.

traits.name: core:ram_filter_feeding
traits.description: Filtering at speed — swimming forward with the mouth held open so tonnes of water pour across the sieve, the way a whale shark or a baleen whale crops entire clouds of plankton in a single glide. The biggest animals that ever lived eat the smallest, by moving.

traits.name: core:mucus_net_feeding
traits.description: Weaving a disposable trap out of your own slime — spinning a sticky net that clogs with particles, then eating net and catch together, as a larvacean does inside its cast-off mucus houses. The organism drapes the sea in snot and swallows the harvest.

traits.name: core:planktivory
traits.description: Chasing the drifting swarm — picking individual copepods and larvae from the water column by eye rather than sieving blindly, as an anchovy or a young herring does. A life spent eating the ocean's motes.

traits.name: core:coprophagy
traits.description: Mining the leftovers of another gut — feeding on dung for the half-digested nutrients and microbes still in it, as a dung beetle rolls its prize or a rabbit re-eats its own soft pellets. One animal's waste is another's twice-cooked meal.

traits.name: core:necrophagy
traits.description: A specialist of the dead — committed to corpses and only corpses, burying and pickling carcasses in antimicrobial secretions to raise young inside them, as burying beetles and blowfly maggots do. Decay is not a threat here but the entire pantry.

traits.name: core:scavenging
traits.description: Reading the sky and the wind for the recently dead — soaring for hours to find a carcass and stomaching bacteria that would kill most animals, as a vulture or a hyena does. Patience and a cast-iron gut turn other creatures' endings into meals.

traits.name: core:frugivory
traits.description: Eating the bribe plants offer — sugary fruit taken in exchange for carrying seeds far away, as fruit bats and toucans do, painting the forest with what they drop. A diet built entirely on being useful to something rooted.

traits.name: core:nectarivory
traits.description: Living on flower-sugar — hovering to drink nectar through a tongue or proboscis and burning it almost as fast as it's sipped, as a hummingbird or a sunbird does at a metabolic sprint. Sweet fuel for a body that cannot afford to stop.

traits.name: core:palynivory
traits.description: Eating the flower's dust — harvesting protein-rich pollen grains, cracking their tough walls to reach the cytoplasm inside, as bees pack their legs with the stuff. A whole diet mined from what most animals inhale by accident.

traits.name: core:exudativory
traits.description: Tapping the plant's bloodstream — gouging bark to drink welling sap, gum, and resin, as a marmoset gnaws gouge-holes or an aphid plugs a phloem tube and lets pressure do the pumping. A slow, sugary theft from something that cannot run.

traits.name: core:grazing
traits.description: Cropping the ground layer — mowing tough grasses and low herbage in bulk and grinding them flat with ever-growing teeth, as bison and horses sweep across a plain. Abundant, abrasive food eaten by the mouthful, all day, forever.

traits.name: core:browsing
traits.description: Reaching up instead of down — selectively plucking leaves, buds, and shoots from shrubs and trees, as a giraffe strips a thorn branch or a deer nibbles new growth. A pickier, higher, more chemically wary way to eat plants.

traits.name: core:granivory_husking
traits.description: Specializing in the armored calorie — a crushing bill or muscular gizzard that shells hard seeds and nuts other animals can't open, as a hawfinch cracks cherry stones or a parrot dismantles a nut. Concentrated energy, locked behind a shell only a specialist can breach.

traits.name: core:xylophagy
traits.description: Digesting the indigestible — living on wood and lignin, which almost nothing can break down alone, by farming gut microbes that do the chemistry, as termites and shipworms do. Turning fallen forests and sunken hulls into food.

traits.name: core:mycophagy
traits.description: Grazing the decomposers — feeding on mushrooms, molds, and mycelium, tolerating toxins that ward off everything else, as a banana slug or a fungus gnat larva does. Eating the things that eat the dead.

traits.name: core:corallivory
traits.description: Eating the reef alive — rasping or biting living coral to strip its polyps and symbiotic algae, as a parrotfish grinds limestone to sand or a crown-of-thorns starfish everts its stomach over a whole colony. A diet that reshapes the seafloor as it feeds.

traits.name: core:spongivory
traits.description: Eating glass and poison on purpose — specializing in sponges laced with silica spicules and defensive toxins that repel almost everything, as hawksbill turtles and some angelfish do. A meal armored inside and out, cracked only by the committed.

traits.name: core:cellulolytic_symbiosis
traits.description: Renting a chemistry set you can't build — housing a menagerie of gut bacteria and protists that shatter cellulose into sugars you can absorb, the innovation behind every cow, termite, and wood-borer. Whole grazing empires rest on this borrowed enzyme.

traits.name: core:hindgut_fermentation
traits.description: Fermenting on the way out — a vast cecum or colon where microbes finish the job after the stomach, trading efficiency for speed so more can be eaten faster, as in horses and rabbits. The strategy of bulk over thoroughness.

traits.name: core:gastrolith_milling
traits.description: Carrying your own millstones — deliberately swallowing grit and stones that tumble in a muscular gizzard to grind tough plants where teeth are absent, as birds and sauropods did. The animal keeps a rock quarry in its belly.

traits.name: core:geophagy
traits.description: Eating the earth for medicine — deliberately swallowing clay and mineral soil to bind plant toxins or replace scarce salts, as macaws crowd a riverbank clay lick or elephants dig mineral caves. Not food, but the antidote that makes food safe.

traits.name: core:insectivory
traits.description: A life spent chasing small, fast protein — snapping up insects and other tiny invertebrates by the hundreds, as a shrew, a swift, or a tree frog does. The most common way for a small animal to make a living on land.

traits.name: core:myrmecophagy
traits.description: Emptying whole societies at a sitting — a sticky tongue and clawed forelimbs built to breach ant and termite nests and lap up their inhabitants by the thousand, as anteaters, pangolins, and aardvarks do. A specialist that eats colonies, not individuals.

traits.name: core:piscivory
traits.description: Hunting the slippery — specializing in fish, with the strike speed, needle teeth, or spear-bill to catch something built to escape underwater, as a heron, a barracuda, or a gharial does. Prey that flees in three dimensions demands a dedicated predator.

traits.name: core:oophagy
traits.description: Robbing the nursery — a diet of eggs, sometimes with throat bones that saw the shell open and spit it back out, as an egg-eating snake swallows a clutch whole. A shortcut to another animal's entire investment in its young.

traits.name: core:ambush_predation
traits.description: Becoming part of the scenery until the last instant — waiting motionless, sometimes for days, then striking faster than the eye can follow, as a mantis, a trapdoor spider, or an anglerfish does. All the energy spent in one explosive moment.

traits.name: core:pursuit_predation
traits.description: Winning by endurance and speed — running, swimming, or flying prey down over distance until it tires, as a wolf, a tuna, or a peregrine does. A body tuned for the long chase and the closing sprint.

traits.name: core:cooperative_hunting
traits.description: Making a weapon out of the group — coordinating roles, ambushes, and relays to bring down prey no single hunter could, as lion prides, wolf packs, and orca pods do. Intelligence and social bonds turned into a killing tool.

traits.name: core:aggressive_mimicry_lure
traits.description: Wearing bait on your own body — dangling a wriggling lure that mimics a worm or a fish, then swallowing whatever comes to investigate, as an anglerfish's glowing esca or an alligator snapping turtle's tongue does. The predator advertises a meal that is itself.

traits.name: core:venom_predation
traits.description: Chemistry as a weapon — injecting a cocktail that paralyzes or pre-digests prey through fangs, stingers, or harpoon teeth, as vipers, cone snails, and spiders do. A small hunter can now safely subdue something far larger and more dangerous.

traits.name: core:constriction_predation
traits.description: Killing with the whole body — coiling around prey and tightening with every exhale until circulation stops, as a python or boa does without a single bite that matters. Muscle itself becomes the lethal tool.

traits.name: core:suction_feeding
traits.description: Eating by implosion — snapping the mouth open so fast that a vacuum inhales prey whole before it can react, as fish, seahorses, and aquatic salamanders do in milliseconds. Underwater, the fastest way to feed is to make the water do the grabbing.

traits.name: core:extraoral_digestion
traits.description: Digesting the meal outside the body — flooding prey with enzymes and drinking back the liquefied remains, as a spider empties a fly to a husk or a starfish everts its stomach over a clam. The stomach reaches out to the food instead of the reverse.

traits.name: core:durophagy
traits.description: Making a living breaking armor — pavement teeth, crushing claws, or hydraulic jaws that shatter shells, tests, and carapaces, as a mosasaur, an eagle ray, or a stone crab does. A brute-force answer to prey that hides inside stone.

traits.name: core:molluscivory
traits.description: Winkling out the soft from the hard — a specialist in prying, drilling, or hammering mollusks from their shells, as an oystercatcher's chisel-bill or a snail kite's hooked beak does. Not brute crushing but surgical extraction.

traits.name: core:web_trap_building
traits.description: Outsourcing the hunt to architecture — spinning silk snares, digging pit traps, or building filtering nets that catch prey while the builder waits, as orb-weavers, antlions, and net-casting caddisfly larvae do. The animal's cleverness moves out into the world it builds.

traits.name: core:tool_assisted_foraging
traits.description: Extending the body with objects — using rocks to crack shells, twigs to fish out grubs, or sponges to protect a probing snout, as sea otters, crows, and chimpanzees do. Food that was locked away becomes reachable through borrowed leverage.

traits.name: core:sanguivory
traits.description: Drinking the living — feeding on blood drawn from a still-breathing host, with anticoagulant saliva and heat-seeking senses to find a vessel, as vampire bats, mosquitoes, and lampreys do. A meal that walks away and can be tapped again.

traits.name: core:lachryphagy
traits.description: Sipping from the eye — drinking the salt and protein of tears straight from a larger animal's face, as certain moths and bees do at the lids of sleeping birds and grazing cattle. One of nature's most delicate thefts.

traits.name: core:mucophagy
traits.description: Grazing the slime that coats other animals — feeding on the protective mucus of fish and larger hosts, sometimes under the guise of a helpful cleaner, as certain blennies and cleaner-fish mimics do. The host's own armor becomes the meal.

traits.name: core:keratophagy
traits.description: Digesting the near-indigestible protein of feathers, hair, horn, and hoof — cracking the tough disulfide bonds of keratin that defeat almost every gut, as feather lice and some fungi do. Eating the parts of an animal that outlast the animal.

traits.name: core:kleptoparasitism
traits.description: Letting others do the hunting — harassing and robbing successful foragers of their catch rather than finding your own, as frigatebirds and skuas chase seabirds until they drop their fish. Theft as a full-time profession.

traits.name: core:osteophagy
traits.description: Making a meal of the skeleton itself — dropping bones to shatter them and digesting the fatty marrow and mineral within, as a bearded vulture does on the wing, or dissolving bone with rootlike acid, as deep-sea Osedax worms mine whale falls. The last part of a carcass becomes the first choice.

traits.name: core:fungiculture
traits.description: Domesticating a crop before humans ever existed — cutting and hauling substrate to feed cultivated fungus gardens that the colony then eats, as leafcutter ants and ambrosia beetles do in tended, weeded, antibiotic-defended plots. Agriculture, invented by insects.

traits.name: core:photosymbiotic_farming
traits.description: Growing your own food inside your flesh — hosting photosynthetic algae in living tissue and living off the sugars they leak in the light, as reef corals and giant clams do, half-animal and half-garden. The body becomes a greenhouse that eats sunlight.

traits.name: core:chemosymbiotic_feeding
traits.description: Feeding on chemistry no sun ever touched — nourishing a body that has no mouth or gut by farming chemosynthetic bacteria in its tissues, which turn vent sulfide into food, as the giant tube worm Riftia does in the dark. Life fed entirely by the poisons of the deep.

traits.name: core:methanotrophic_feeding
traits.description: Grazing on marsh-gas — hosting symbionts that oxidize methane welling up from cold seeps and turning that flammable breath into flesh, as seep mussels do at hydrocarbon vents. A diet of the gas that bubbles from a buried, rotting world.

traits.name: core:sulfur_mat_grazing
traits.description: Mowing the chemical lawn of the deep — rasping thick mats of sulfur-oxidizing bacteria that carpet vents and seeps, as some snails and worms do where the sun never reaches. A pasture built not on light but on brimstone.

traits.name: core:lithophagy
traits.description: Eating stone as substrate, not seasoning — boring into and ingesting rock for the organic films and minerals bound inside it, as endolithic borers and some worms tunnel through solid limestone. On the right world, the ground itself is the meal.

traits.name: core:silicate_digestion
traits.description: Dissolving the bones of a rocky world — secreting acids and enzymes that pry usable elements from silicate minerals, so quartz and basalt become groceries. An alien lithovore that treats a cliff face the way a grazer treats a meadow.

traits.name: core:metallophagy
traits.description: Living where metal is food — concentrating and metabolizing heavy-metal ions that would poison anything else, thriving in ore veins and toxic springs the way hyperaccumulator plants hint at on Earth. An alien palate for copper, arsenic, and rarer things.

traits.name: core:electrophagy
traits.description: Feeding on voltage itself — tapping electrical potential from mineral batteries in the crust or bleeding charge from the nervous systems of living prey, sensing the current the way a shark senses a heartbeat and rasping it away as energy. A predator of the flow of electrons.

traits.name: core:radiotrophy_grazing
traits.description: Grazing on radiation as if it were sunlight — carpeting hot rock and reactor-glow ruins with melanin-black pigment that harvests ionizing energy, the way Chernobyl's fungi already lean toward the source. A pasture animal for worlds where the danger is the food.

traits.name: core:thermal_gradient_feeding
traits.description: A living thermocouple grazing the seams between hot and cold — straddling a steep temperature boundary and drawing usable energy from the heat trying to cross it, so a vent's edge or a day-night terminator becomes a feeding ground. An organism that eats the flow of warmth.

traits.name: core:gigantophagy
traits.description: Specializing in prey that dwarfs the hunter — bringing down or dismantling megafauna many times one's own mass through coordination, venom, or sheer relentlessness, as pack-hunting predators fell the largest herbivores or a whale-fall community consumes a leviathan for decades. A diet measured in giants.

traits.name: core:distance_chemoreception
traits.description: Faint plumes of scent drifting on the current become a map of the unseen. Like a moth reading a female's pheromone from a mile downwind, this sense pulls meaning out of molecules that arrive from far away.

traits.name: core:contact_chemoreception
traits.description: To touch is to taste. Chemical sensors on skin, feet, or feelers read the surface of whatever the creature brushes against, the way a butterfly drums a leaf with its legs to decide if it is food.

traits.name: core:co2_chemoreception
traits.description: A rising tide of carbon dioxide betrays breath, blood, and living warmth nearby. Mosquitoes home in on exhaled CO2 from meters away; this sense turns a colorless gas into a hunting cue.

traits.name: core:oxygen_chemosensing
traits.description: The creature feels the thinning and thickening of dissolved oxygen the way a diver feels their lungs. It flees suffocating dead water and seeks the bright, breathable shallows.

traits.name: core:ph_acid_sensing
traits.description: Sourness and sting register as a distinct warning channel. Acid-sensing ion channels flare when the water turns caustic, letting the organism recoil from corrosive vents and rotting muck.

traits.name: core:osmoreception_salinity
traits.description: The organism tastes the saltiness of its world and the balance of its own fluids. Like a salmon detecting the freshwater signature of its natal river, it navigates gradients of brine and rain.

traits.name: core:vomeronasal_pheromone
traits.description: A second nose, tuned only to the language of its own kind. Like a snake flicking its tongue to the Jacobson's organ, it reads courtship, alarm, and territory written in airborne pheromones.

traits.name: core:ultraviolet_vision
traits.description: A whole band of the world lit up that we never see. Flowers blaze with hidden nectar guides and urine trails glow along the ground, as reindeer and kestrels perceive the ultraviolet.

traits.name: core:color_trichromacy
traits.description: Three kinds of cone divide the light into a rich weave of hue. The drab world separates into ripe red, poisonous yellow, and cool green, the way primates pick fruit from a tangle of leaves.

traits.name: core:spectral_hyperacuity
traits.description: A dozen or more classes of photoreceptor, each a narrow window onto the spectrum. Like a mantis shrimp's alien eyes, it reads color as a barcode scanned in a single glance, from deep ultraviolet to the far red.

traits.name: core:infrared_thermal_imaging
traits.description: The brain fuses signals from many heat-pits into a true image of warmth, overlaid on the visual world. In full darkness the creature watches prey burn bright against a cold landscape.

traits.name: core:compound_eyes
traits.description: Hundreds of tiny lenses tile a bulging dome, each capturing one shard of the scene. Like a dragonfly, the creature stitches a near-panoramic mosaic exquisitely tuned to the faintest flicker of motion.

traits.name: core:pinhole_eye
traits.description: A dark cup with a tiny aperture, forming a dim true image with no lens at all. Like the living pinhole camera of a nautilus, it trades sharpness for the simple ability to know where things are.

traits.name: core:tapetum_lucidum
traits.description: A mirror behind the retina bounces stray photons back for a second chance to be caught. This is the eerie eyeshine of a cat in headlights, doubling the harvest of light on the darkest night.

traits.name: core:scotopic_night_vision
traits.description: An eye packed with hypersensitive rod cells drinks in the faintest starlight. Like an owl gliding through moonless woods, the creature moves surefooted where others are struck blind.
